proposals.title: Distributed Consensus of Nonlinear Agents under Joint Topology and Finite Data Rate Constraints: A Hybrid Event-Triggered Control Framework
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on networked control systems has extensively studied data rate limits for consensus, but primarily for linear agent dynamics. Real-world multi-agent systems, such as UAV swarms and robotic teams, exhibit inherent nonlinearities. The interplay between communication topology, limited bandwidth, and nonlinear dynamics remains an open problem, crucial for deploying resilient distributed control in bandwidth-constrained environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a class of Lipschitz nonlinear agent dynamics, a co-design of distributed event-triggered controllers and dynamic quantization schemes can guarantee practical consensus under a joint constraint defined by the network's algebraic connectivity and a finite data rate per channel.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative distance-based positioning framework to general nonlinear dynamics. The method integrates: 1) A distributed event-triggered controller, where agents broadcast quantized state measurements only when a locally computed error exceeds a topology-dependent threshold, reducing unnecessary transmissions. 2) A dynamic quantization protocol that adjusts the quantization range and coarseness based on the event-triggering condition and estimated consensus error. 3) Lyapunov and input-to-state stability tools will be used to jointly analyze the effects of nonlinearity, triggering, and quantization, deriving sufficient conditions on the required data rate relative to network topology parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for guaranteeing consensus of nonlinear multi-agent systems under explicit joint topology-data rate constraints. It will yield explicit, computable bounds on the required bit-rate per link as a function of network connectivity and agent nonlinearity, directly informing the design of communication protocols for nonlinear networked systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the rigorous extension of data-rate-limited consensus theory from linear to nonlinear agent dynamics, and the explicit co-design of event-triggering and dynamic quantization that is jointly sensitive to both the communication graph and the system's nonlinearity, a step beyond prior linear or topology-agnostic approaches.

proposals.title: Robust Distributed Consensus for Multi-Agent Systems with Heterogeneous Model Uncertainties: An H-infinity and Relative Measurement Framework
proposals.motivation: Distributed consensus control for multi-agent systems (MAS) is critical for applications like UAV swarms and sensor networks, but real-world agents often have heterogeneous and uncertain dynamics, leading to performance degradation or instability. Existing consensus protocols, including those based on relative positioning, frequently assume perfect agent models, limiting their practical robustness. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need to integrate advanced robust control theory with distributed frameworks to guarantee reliable coordination under significant model perturbations.
proposals.hypothesis: By synthesizing distributed consensus protocols using H-infinity and mu-synthesis techniques, explicitly incorporating relative state measurements and local error dynamics, we can achieve guaranteed performance and stability for MAS with bounded, heterogeneous dynamic uncertainties.
proposals.method: We will formulate the consensus error dynamics of a networked MAS, where each agent's nominal model is augmented with multiplicative or additive uncertainty representations. Using only relative position/velocity measurements (aligned with the researcher's prior work), we will design a structured, distributed controller. The core technical approach involves setting up a linear fractional transformation (LFT) model of the networked system and employing mu-synthesis (DK-iteration) to minimize the structured singular value, thereby optimizing robust performance. The synthesis will explicitly handle the communication graph topology as part of the interconnection structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally verifiable design methodology for robust consensus protocols that tolerate a specified class of agent dynamic uncertainties. It will bridge the gap between theoretical robust control (H-infinity/mu) and practical distributed multi-agent coordination, offering explicit performance bounds. The resulting framework will extend the applicability of relative-measurement-based control to more realistic, heterogeneous agent fleets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of mu-synthesis for structured robustness analysis into the design of distributed consensus protocols that rely solely on relative local measurements, a combination not extensively explored in pre-2026 literature. This moves beyond typical Lyapunov-based robust designs by providing less conservative, frequency-domain guarantees for networked uncertain systems.

proposals.title: Distributed Topology Synthesis for Guaranteed Consensus under Fixed Communication Rate Constraints
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent systems, consensus is often studied assuming a given network topology. However, in bandwidth-limited applications like UAV swarms, the communication data rate is a fixed, scarce resource that constrains the topology. The inverse problem—designing a network topology to guarantee consensus given agent dynamics and a fixed rate—is crucial for resource-aware system design but remains underexplored. This directly addresses the challenge of deploying reliable distributed control under strict communication budgets.
proposals.hypothesis: For a class of linear agent dynamics under a fixed, quantized data rate per communication link, there exists a constructive method to design a sparse or time-varying communication topology that guarantees asymptotic consensus, where the topology is a function of the rate constraint and the agents' inherent coupling strength.
proposals.method: We will model agents with double-integrator dynamics communicating quantized relative state measurements. The core method involves two layers: first, a spectral constraint analysis linking the algebraic connectivity of the graph Laplacian to a rate-dependent consensus error bound. Second, we will formulate a topology design optimization as a mixed-integer semi-definite program (MISDP) to minimize edge count while satisfying the spectral constraint derived from the rate. For scalability, we will develop a greedy, distributed heuristic inspired by 'Distributed Framework Matching' that iteratively activates or prunes links based on local gradient estimates of the global spectral gap.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, optimization-based framework for co-designing network topology and constrained communication protocols for consensus. It will yield explicit design rules linking data rate, agent dynamics, and minimal connectivity, advancing the field of resource-aware distributed control. The algorithms will enable practitioners to certify consensus for a given hardware communication limit.
proposals.novelty: While most pre-2026 work adjusts control laws to a given topology or adapts quantization to a fixed graph, we invert the problem: the topology becomes the design variable conditioned on a hard communication rate. This integrates spectral graph theory with information-theoretic constraints in a novel synthesis problem.

proposals.title: A Distributed Robust Filter for Nonlinear Multi-Agent Systems with Mixed Deterministic and Stochastic Uncertainties
proposals.motivation: Distributed control and estimation for multi-agent systems, such as UAV swarms, often rely on linearized dynamics or assume purely stochastic noise, limiting their applicability to real-world nonlinear systems with complex uncertainties. Pre-2026, there is a significant gap in robust filtering frameworks that can simultaneously handle inherent nonlinearities, bounded deterministic disturbances (e.g., wind gusts, model mismatch), and stochastic measurement noise in a distributed setting, which is critical for resilient collaborative tasks like target encirclement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a nonlinear observer structure with a distributed, relative-measurement-based consensus protocol and a hybrid uncertainty characterization, we can achieve stable, accurate state estimation for each agent despite the mixed uncertainties and nonlinear dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a distributed filter architecture where each agent runs a local estimator based on a nonlinear system model (e.g., quadrotor dynamics). The core algorithm will combine: 1) a modified Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) to handle stochastic noise and nonlinearities locally, 2) a deterministic robust control term, designed via Linear Matrix Inequalities (LMIs) or H-infinity principles, to attenuate bounded disturbances, and 3) a consensus update step using only relative local measurements (e.g., relative distance, bearing) to fuse information from neighboring agents, ensuring convergence of estimates across the network.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical filtering framework that relaxes the linearity assumption for distributed multi-agent systems under mixed uncertainties. It will provide rigorous stability and performance bounds for the proposed hybrid filter. The results will directly advance the robustness of distributed control strategies for applications like resilient formation control and cooperative target tracking.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of a stochastic nonlinear filter (UKF) with a deterministic robust controller and a distributed consensus protocol specifically designed for relative local measurements, creating a unified solution for a problem typically addressed with separate, simplifying assumptions.

proposals.title: Distributed Robust Filtering for Multi-Agent Localization in GPS-Denied Industrial Environments
proposals.motivation: Multi-agent systems in industrial settings, such as warehouse logistics or collaborative inspection, often operate in GPS-denied environments with significant structured uncertainties, including dynamic obstacles, magnetic interference, and unreliable anchor positions. Existing distributed filters, which often assume Gaussian noise and known models, degrade severely under these conditions, leading to localization failures and compromised coordination. Validating robust filtering methods on real hardware is critical to bridge the gap between theoretical resilience and practical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed robust filter, integrating relative distance-based positioning with a novel adaptive bound on structured uncertainties, will maintain sub-meter localization accuracy for a team of quadrotor UAVs in a cluttered, anchor-limited environment, where standard distributed Kalman filters fail. This performance will enable sustained collision-resilient behaviors without external positioning aids.
proposals.method: We will extend our prior relative positioning and neural antisynchronization control framework by designing a distributed H-infinity inspired filter. Each agent will fuse local IMU measurements with intermittent relative range measurements (from UWB) and bearing data, modeling anchor position drift and non-line-of-sight errors as bounded, structured uncertainties. The filter's robustness gain will be adapted online via a lightweight neural network, trained via deep reinforcement learning to minimize a consensus-based estimation error cost. The integrated system will be tested on a fleet of 3-5 collision-resilient quadrotors in a warehouse mockup with moving obstacles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first real-world validations of a distributed robust filter for multi-agent systems under significant, structured non-Gaussian uncertainties. It will yield a publicly available dataset of challenging flight trajectories with ground truth. The results will establish practical guidelines for tuning robustness versus convergence speed in anchor-limited scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of an adaptive robust filter with a relative positioning framework specifically for real-world structured uncertainties (e.g., systematic anchor errors), moving beyond standard Gaussian or bounded-but-unstructured assumptions. The online adaptation of robustness parameters based on local consensus error is a new approach for practical distributed estimation.

proposals.title: Distributed Robust State Estimation for Multi-Agent Systems with Physical and Relative-Measurement Constraints
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent systems for tasks like target encirclement or resilient formation control, agents must estimate their states (e.g., position, velocity) using noisy relative measurements while respecting inherent physical constraints (e.g., maximum velocity, safe operational boundaries). Standard robust Kalman filters (like H∞ or set-membership filters) handle measurement noise and bounded disturbances but often treat state constraints as secondary, post-hoc corrections, which can degrade estimation consistency and robustness in safety-critical distributed operations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly integrating hard physical state constraints and relative-measurement models directly into the robust Kalman filtering prediction and update steps will yield more accurate, consistent, and physically admissible state estimates for each agent, thereby improving the performance of downstream distributed control laws.
proposals.method: We will develop a distributed constrained robust Kalman filter (DCRKF). Each agent will run a local filter that fuses its proprioceptive data with relative measurements from neighbors (e.g., distance, bearing). The core innovation is a two-layer update: first, a robust H∞-type gain is computed to minimize worst-case estimation error from bounded disturbances; second, this preliminary estimate is projected onto the feasible set defined by linear and nonlinear state/parameter constraints (e.g., via quadratic programming or a moving horizon estimation framework). The framework will leverage the agent's local controller and the distributed communication topology from Xie's prior work to share necessary estimation parameters without central coordination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, distributed estimation framework that guarantees state estimates remain within physically realistic bounds, enhancing the safety and reliability of multi-agent control systems. It will bridge the gap between robust estimation theory and constrained optimization, offering a new tool for applications in collision-resilient UAV swarms and cooperative target tracking.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, online integration of robust worst-case estimation (for disturbance rejection) with hard constraint satisfaction within a distributed, relative-measurement-based architecture, moving beyond the common practice of applying constraints as an separate, ad-hoc correction step.

proposals.title: Distributed Robust Filter Synthesis for Multi-Agent Systems via Relative-Measurement-Based Linear Matrix Inequalities
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent systems performing tasks like target encirclement or resilient formation control, agents often rely on noisy relative measurements (e.g., distance, bearing) for localization. Pre-2026 robust control theory provides powerful Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI) tools for handling model uncertainty and disturbances, but their application to distributed filter design, particularly for networks with only relative sensing, remains underexplored. This gap limits the performance and reliability of distributed estimation in practical, uncertain environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed H-infinity or H2 filter, synthesized via a novel LMI formulation that explicitly incorporates the structure of relative measurement graphs and bounded inter-agent uncertainties, will guarantee robust stability and superior estimation performance compared to standard Kalman-type filters in the presence of measurement noise and model errors.
proposals.method: The method will extend LMI-based robust control synthesis to the distributed filtering problem. First, we will model the multi-agent system's error dynamics, where each agent's state is estimated using a local filter that fuses its own kinematics with relative measurements from neighbors. Second, we will formulate the filter design as a convex optimization problem with LMI constraints derived from bounded real lemma or H2 performance criteria, explicitly encoding the network's Laplacian and relative output matrices. Third, we will solve these LMIs using standard pre-2026 solvers (e.g., YALMIP/SDPT3) to obtain the distributed filter gains that minimize the worst-case estimation error. The synthesis will be validated in simulation for a UAV swarm scenario.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal, computationally tractable framework for synthesizing robust distributed filters directly from a network's relative sensing model. It will provide guaranteed performance bounds for estimation under uncertainty, enhancing the reliability of distributed control protocols for multi-agent systems. The results will bridge robust control theory and distributed estimation, offering a new tool for researchers in networked control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tailored integration of robust LMI techniques with the specific algebraic structure of relative-measurement-based multi-agent networks, moving beyond centralized or idealized measurement models to address a core challenge in distributed sensing and control.

proposals.title: Distributed Consensus Filtering for Multi-Agent Target Tracking with Relative-Only Measurements
proposals.motivation: Multi-agent systems for tasks like target encirclement and collision-resilient flight, as studied by Xie, rely on accurate, shared situational awareness. In GPS-denied or communication-limited environments, agents often possess only noisy, local relative measurements (e.g., distances, bearings). A centralized filter is a single point of failure and requires high-bandwidth communication, which is impractical for scalable, resilient networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed consensus Kalman filtering architecture, where agents fuse local relative measurements and iteratively reach agreement on a common state estimate via peer-to-peer communication, will provide accurate, resilient tracking performance comparable to a centralized filter while drastically reducing communication overhead.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative distance-based positioning framework from Xie's prior work into a formal distributed estimation architecture. Each agent will run a local Kalman filter based on its relative measurements. A consensus algorithm (e.g., consensus on information form or the diffusion strategy) will be layered atop these local filters, allowing agents to iteratively average information vectors and matrices with their neighbors over a time-varying communication graph. The stability and convergence of the proposed filter will be analyzed using Lyapunov methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, distributed filtering framework tailored for the relative-measurement models common in multi-robot systems. It will yield a rigorous analysis of estimation performance under communication constraints and offer a practical algorithm for resilient cooperative perception, directly applicable to Xie's research on distributed control for target encirclement and collision resilience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of a relative measurement model (inherently local and nonlinear) with a discrete-time consensus protocol within a stable distributed Kalman filter, specifically designed for the cooperative control scenarios central to Xie's research portfolio, rather than generic state estimation.

proposals.title: A Distributed, Robust Fixed-Lag Smoother for Multi-Agent Systems with Uncertain Dynamics and Relative Measurements
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent systems for tasks like target encirclement or resilient formation flight, agents rely on noisy relative measurements (e.g., distances, bearings) and have uncertain dynamics. Standard Kalman smoothers are fragile to these uncertainties and centralized processing is a single point of failure. A distributed, robust smoother is needed to provide accurate, retrospective state estimates that enhance situational awareness and control performance for the entire team.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating robustness concepts from H-infinity or set-membership filtering with a distributed, consensus-based fusion architecture, we can develop a fixed-lag smoother that provides more accurate and reliable retrospective state estimates for networked agents under model uncertainty and local measurement noise than existing distributed filters or non-robust smoothers.
proposals.method: We will extend the robustness frameworks from the researcher's prior work on neural antisynchronization control and collision resilience to the smoothing problem. The core method is a distributed fixed-lag smoother where each agent runs a local robust filter (e.g., a robust information filter) and exchanges information matrices and vectors with neighbors, as in the 'Distributed Framework Matching' concept. A consensus protocol will fuse these local estimates over a sliding window of recent time steps, ensuring all agents converge to a common, smoothed state trajectory that is robust to bounded disturbances and model errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel distributed smoothing algorithm that is provably robust to uncertainties, 2) a theoretical analysis of its convergence and robustness bounds, and 3) validation in simulation for multi-UAV scenarios using relative distance-based positioning, demonstrating improved retrospective trajectory accuracy for cooperative control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of robust control concepts into a distributed smoothing framework, specifically for multi-agent systems using only relative measurements. Prior distributed smoothers often assume perfect models or are not designed for the specific uncertainty structures common in agile mobile agents.

proposals.title: Dual Riccati-Based Robust Control for Multi-Agent Systems with Relative-Only Measurements
proposals.motivation: Distributed multi-agent systems for tasks like target encirclement or resilient formation control must operate robustly against model uncertainties and disturbances using limited sensing, such as relative distance measurements. Pre-2026 robust control methods often impose centralized computation or lack scalability, while distributed estimation techniques are mature. The well-known duality between optimal control (LQR) and optimal estimation (Kalman filter) Riccati equations suggests a powerful but underexplored pathway to synthesize distributed robust controllers directly from distributed estimators.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed robust controller for a network of agents can be synthesized by exploiting the mathematical duality with a distributed estimation Riccati equation, leading to guaranteed performance and stability bounds under model uncertainties using only relative local measurements.
proposals.method: We will formalize the duality for a network of agents with linearized dynamics subject to disturbances. Starting from a distributed Kalman consensus filter (as used in relative positioning), we will derive its associated estimation Riccati equation. By applying the duality principle, we will transform this into a corresponding control Riccati equation to synthesize a distributed H-infinity or LQR-like controller. The control law will be implemented locally using only relative state information from neighbors, and stability will be analyzed using Lyapunov methods and small-gain theorems for interconnected systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, duality-driven framework for designing distributed robust controllers, directly linking estimation and control architectures. It will yield a verifiable, scalable control law that enhances the robustness of multi-agent encirclement and collision-resilient behaviors against uncertainties, advancing beyond ad-hoc or centralized robust designs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly leveraging the Riccati duality to derive a *distributed* robust controller from a *distributed* estimator, a synthesis not fully explored in pre-2026 multi-agent literature, which typically treats estimation and control separately or applies duality only in centralized settings.

proposals.title: Distributed Hybrid Control for Multi-Agent Encirclement with Partially Known Dynamics via Model-Based Priors and Neural Synchronization
proposals.motivation: Distributed control for multi-agent systems, such as UAV target encirclement, often assumes completely unknown agent dynamics, relying heavily on data-intensive model-free reinforcement learning (RL). This leads to poor sample efficiency and slow convergence in real-world applications. Leveraging partial prior knowledge, which is often available from basic physics or manufacturer specifications, can significantly accelerate learning while retaining adaptability to unmodeled disturbances and inter-agent couplings.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a known nominal dynamics model as a controller prior within a distributed neural synchronization framework will reduce the sample complexity and improve the transient performance of learning-based encirclement control, compared to purely model-free approaches.
proposals.method: We will design a hybrid controller for each agent, decomposing the control law into a model-based component from the known nominal dynamics and a neural network-based adaptive component. The neural component will be trained online using a relative distance-based positioning scheme, inspired by Xie's prior work, with an antisynchronization objective to achieve stable encirclement. The learning will be guided by a Lyapunov-based stability analysis that incorporates the model prior, ensuring safe exploration and faster convergence of the neural compensator.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework for integrating partial model knowledge into distributed learning-based control, specifically for cooperative tasks like encirclement. It will yield a practical control strategy with demonstrably faster deployment and greater reliability for multi-robot systems, bridging the gap between model-based and model-free paradigms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of relative positioning-based neural antisynchronization control with a structured model-based prior in a distributed setting, moving beyond the common 'black-box' RL assumption while avoiding the fragility of full model dependence.

proposals.title: Distributed Prescribed Performance Control for Multi-Agent Systems with Explicit State and Actuation Constraints
proposals.motivation: Prescribed Performance Control (PPC) is a powerful pre-2026 method for guaranteeing transient and steady-state performance bounds in multi-agent systems. However, standard PPC transformations do not explicitly account for hard physical constraints, such as actuator saturation or collision-avoidance safety margins. This gap limits its application to safety-critical systems like UAV swarms, where constraint satisfaction is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating explicit, hard constraints on agent states and control inputs directly into the PPC transformation and distributed control law synthesis, we can achieve guaranteed prescribed performance while rigorously ensuring all operational constraints are never violated.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative distance-based positioning framework from Xie's prior work. The core method involves designing a novel, constrained performance function and error transformation that inherently respects predefined state constraints (e.g., minimum inter-agent distances). For input constraints, we will synthesize a distributed controller using a Barrier Lyapunov Function (BLF) approach, ensuring the transformed error dynamics remain within a safe set. The control law will be implemented locally by each agent using only relative measurements, maintaining the distributed nature of the framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified distributed control framework that rigorously enforces both performance specifications and safety constraints. It will deliver a set of sufficient conditions for stability and constraint satisfaction under the PPC paradigm, directly applicable to encirclement and formation tasks with non-ideal, real-world agents.
proposals.novelty: The integration of hard constraints into the PPC transformation itself, rather than treating them as separate, subsequent considerations, is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond existing PPC or constrained control methods, which typically address these requirements in isolation.

proposals.title: Distributed Formation Control for Multi-Agent Systems under General Directed Graphs via Relative Error and Neural Synchronization
proposals.motivation: Most existing distributed control strategies for multi-agent systems, including Xie's prior work on relative distance-based positioning, rely on strong connectivity or detailed balance assumptions for the communication graph. In real-world applications like UAV swarms with asymmetric communication links or sensor failures, these assumptions are often violated, limiting the applicability of current methods. Extending control schemes to general directed topologies, including those that are not strongly connected, is a critical open challenge for robust decentralized coordination.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a distributed observer for estimating a common reference from local relative measurements under a general directed graph with a neural network-based antisynchronization controller, a group of agents can achieve a desired formation even without global connectivity guarantees.
proposals.method: We will first design a distributed protocol for agents to estimate a common reference signal using only local, relative state information from neighbors, building on Xie's 'Distributed Framework Matching' but relaxing connectivity requirements. This estimated reference will feed into a relative error-based formation controller. The core innovation is augmenting this with a neural network-based antisynchronization control law, inspired by Xie's prior neural antisynchronization work, to handle the nonlinear dynamics and stabilize the system under the information flow constraints of a general directed graph. Stability will be proven using Lyapunov methods and graph-theoretic analysis for directed networks containing a directed spanning tree.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, provably stable control framework for multi-agent formation under the weakest possible connectivity condition—the existence of a directed spanning tree. It will directly extend the applicability of relative measurement-based control to a broader class of realistic network topologies. The integration of neural antisynchronization with distributed reference estimation offers a new pathway for robust control in information-constrained environments.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the combined use of a distributed observer for general directed graphs with a neural antisynchronization controller to achieve formation control, moving beyond the strong connectivity or undirected graph assumptions prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Robust Distributed Encirclement and Formation Control Under Switching Communication Topologies via Relative Distance-Based Positioning and Neural Adaptive Controllers
proposals.motivation: In real-world multi-agent systems, such as UAV swarms or mobile sensor networks, communication links are prone to intermittent failures, obstructions, and deliberate reconfiguration, leading to dynamically switching network topologies. Pre-2026 distributed control methods often assume fixed or connected graphs, limiting their practical applicability. Investigating robustness under topology switches is crucial for deploying reliable autonomous systems in adversarial or complex environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a distributed framework matching mechanism with a neural adaptive antisynchronization controller, a multi-agent system can maintain stable encirclement and formation control objectives even under arbitrary switching of communication links, provided the union of graphs over a bounded interval retains connectivity.
proposals.method: The method extends the researcher's prior relative distance-based positioning framework. We will model the communication network as a time-varying graph with switching signals. A distributed consensus-based estimator will be used for target state estimation under topology changes. The core neural antisynchronization controller will be augmented with an adaptive law to compensate for switching-induced errors, using Lyapunov-based stability analysis to derive sufficient conditions on switching frequency (average dwell-time) for convergence. Validation will involve numerical simulations and hardware experiments with quadrotor UAVs subject to pseudo-random link failures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally analyzed control framework for multi-agent encirclement and formation tasks that is provably robust to communication topology switches. It will deliver explicit practical stability bounds (dwell-time) for system designers. The integration of neural adaptive control with graph-theoretic conditions will bridge a gap between machine learning-based control and classical multi-agent systems theory.
proposals.novelty: While switching topologies have been studied for consensus, their integration with the specific problem of noncooperative target encirclement using relative-distance feedback and neural antisynchronization control is novel. The combination of a framework matching protocol for role assignment with an adaptive neural controller to handle switching disturbances represents a significant advance over static-topology approaches.

proposals.title: Distributed Consensus-Based Optimization for Adaptive Parameter Tuning in Multi-Agent Formation Control
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent systems performing tasks like target encirclement or resilient formation control, optimal performance depends on controller parameters (e.g., gains, thresholds) that are typically tuned centrally or heuristically. This is inflexible in dynamic, communication-limited environments and does not scale. A distributed, online optimization method is needed to allow agents to autonomously and cooperatively adapt their parameters using only local interactions and relative measurements.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a consensus-based distributed optimization layer with existing relative-positioning controllers will enable a network of agents to collectively and efficiently converge to near-optimal controller parameters, improving task performance metrics (e.g., formation error, energy use) compared to static or independently tuned parameters.
proposals.method: We will extend Dr. Xie's prior work on relative distance-based positioning and neural antisynchronization control. A distributed gradient descent algorithm, such as DIGing or EXTRA, will be implemented as a secondary consensus layer. Each agent will compute a local cost based on its tracking error and control effort from its primary controller. Agents will exchange these cost gradients with neighbors via the 'Distributed Framework Matching' protocol to perform consensus-based optimization, iteratively tuning their local controller parameters. The approach will be validated in simulation for UAV collision resilience and multi-target encirclement scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable framework for online distributed parameter tuning in multi-agent control, reducing reliance on centralized design. It will demonstrate concrete performance gains in formation accuracy and resilience. The integration strategy will serve as a template for merging distributed optimization with nonlinear, measurement-based control laws.
proposals.novelty: While distributed optimization and multi-agent control are mature fields, their tight integration for real-time, model-free tuning of nonlinear controller parameters using only relative local measurements is novel. This moves beyond using consensus for state estimation or task allocation to directly optimize the control policy itself in a distributed manner.

proposals.title: Prescribed Performance Control for a Single Agent with Unknown Dynamics and Output Constraints via Neural Approximation and Error Transformation
proposals.motivation: Prescribed Performance Control (PPC) is a powerful framework for guaranteeing transient and steady-state performance bounds, but its application often assumes known system dynamics or relies on multi-agent coordination for robustness. For a single agent with completely unknown dynamics and strict output constraints—common in safety-critical applications like medical robotics or autonomous systems—existing PPC methods can fail or require overly conservative designs. A simplified, robust single-agent solution is needed to broaden PPC's practical applicability.
proposals.hypothesis: A control law integrating a neural network approximator with a transformed error surface derived from prescribed performance functions can achieve guaranteed output constraint satisfaction and asymptotic tracking for a single agent with unknown, nonlinear dynamics.
proposals.method: The method will adapt the relative distance-based error transformation from the researcher's multi-agent work to define a scalar performance envelope for the single agent's output tracking error. A Radial Basis Function Neural Network (RBFNN) will be employed to approximate the unknown system dynamics online. The controller will be synthesized via backstepping, using the transformed error and the NN approximation to derive an adaptive control law that ensures the transformed error system is stable, thereby enforcing the original prescribed performance bounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a simplified, standalone PPC framework for single-agent systems, reducing the algorithmic and computational complexity associated with distributed PPC. It will formally prove stability and performance guarantees under dynamics uncertainty, providing a foundational controller that can be deployed on resource-constrained platforms. This bridges a gap between theoretical multi-agent PPC and practical single-agent applications.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate simplification and specialization of a multi-agent, relative-measurement-based PPC framework to a single agent, replacing inter-agent coordination with a direct neural approximation of unknown dynamics while rigorously preserving the prescribed performance guarantees. This creates a new, lightweight archetype for constrained control of isolated systems.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Distributed Control for Large-Scale Multi-Agent Systems via Cluster-Level Relative Positioning and Neural Anti-Synchronization
proposals.motivation: The scalability of distributed multi-agent control frameworks is a critical challenge for applications like massive drone swarms or sensor networks. Existing methods, including the researcher's prior work on relative distance-based control, face computational and communication bottlenecks when directly applied to hundreds or thousands of agents. A hierarchical approach, inspired by biological and organizational systems, is a promising but underexplored path to achieve scalable, resilient coordination.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-layer hierarchical control architecture, where intra-cluster coordination is managed by relative distance-based neural anti-synchronization and inter-cluster coordination is governed by a meta-controller using aggregated cluster states, will maintain system stability and performance objectives while significantly reducing communication overhead and computational complexity in very large-scale systems.
proposals.method: We will extend the researcher's relative distance-based positioning and neural anti-synchronization control to operate within dynamically defined agent clusters. A higher-level distributed meta-controller, informed by the 'Distributed Framework Matching' concept, will coordinate these clusters by treating each as a virtual agent with a state defined by its centroid and spatial extent. The meta-controller will set reference trajectories for clusters, while local controllers within each cluster enforce formation and collision resilience, using only local relative measurements. Stability will be analyzed using Lyapunov methods and cluster-level error dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable control architecture for massive multi-agent systems, formally bridging fine-grained agent-level control with coarse-grained cluster-level coordination. It will deliver theoretical guarantees on hierarchical system stability and demonstrate practical feasibility through simulation of systems an order of magnitude larger than typical flat architectures can handle efficiently.
proposals.novelty: The integration of neural anti-synchronization for intra-cluster dynamics with a lightweight, matching-based meta-controller for inter-cluster coordination is a novel synthesis, moving beyond flat distributed control or simple cluster-head models to a truly hierarchical, resilience-inheriting design.

proposals.title: Distributed Containment Control with Multiple Leaders via Relative Distance-Based Positioning and Prescribed Performance Functions
proposals.motivation: Containment control, where follower agents converge to a convex hull defined by multiple leaders, is critical for applications like multi-target surveillance and safe escorting of UAV swarms. Existing pre-2026 methods often assume perfect communication or lack explicit, verifiable guarantees on transient performance (e.g., convergence rate, steady-state error bounds), which is essential for safety in cluttered environments. This work addresses the gap for scenarios with multiple dynamic leaders using only local relative measurements.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed control law, integrating a relative distance-based positioning mechanism with a prescribed performance function (PPF), can guarantee that all followers achieve containment within the leaders' dynamic convex hull while strictly respecting predefined bounds on convergence speed and ultimate positioning error.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative distance-based positioning framework from Xie's prior work on target encirclement to the containment problem. Each follower will use only local relative measurements to neighboring followers and leaders to estimate the required containment vector. A decentralized neural network-based controller will be designed to drive the followers, with the tracking error transformed via a PPF to enforce prescribed transient and steady-state performance. Stability will be proven using Lyapunov theory and the properties of the neural approximator for unknown dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a rigorously proven distributed containment control scheme with explicit, user-defined performance guarantees, enhancing operational safety and predictability. It will extend the applicability of prescribed performance control from consensus and tracking problems to the more complex multi-leader containment scenario using a relative sensing paradigm.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a relative distance-based positioning strategy—typically used for encirclement—with prescribed performance control for multi-leader containment, ensuring verifiable transient performance without requiring global position information or a centralized coordinator.

proposals.title: Distributed Robust Encirclement Control with Relative Measurement-Based State Estimation Error Bounds
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent systems for tasks like target encirclement, controllers often assume perfect state knowledge. However, in real-world deployments, agents rely on noisy relative measurements (e.g., from vision or UWB) for localization, leading to estimation errors that degrade performance and stability. Existing distributed control frameworks largely treat estimation and control separately, lacking a unified design that explicitly accounts for the bounds of these errors to guarantee robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling the worst-case bounds of state estimation errors derived from relative measurements and integrating these bounds into the controller synthesis, we can design a distributed control law that guarantees robust encirclement and collision avoidance even with imperfect state information.
proposals.method: We will extend a relative distance-based positioning framework with a local, nonlinear observer (e.g., a high-gain observer or sliding mode observer) to generate state estimates and associated error bounds. These bounds will be used to synthesize a distributed robust controller, likely using a neural antisynchronization control structure augmented with input-to-state stability (ISS) or barrier Lyapunov functions. The controller will dynamically adjust agent behavior based on the real-time confidence in the state estimate, ensuring safety and task completion under bounded estimation uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, integrated estimation-control co-design methodology for distributed multi-agent systems using relative measurements. It will deliver provable robustness guarantees for encirclement tasks under realistic sensing constraints, advancing beyond the perfect-state-information paradigm. The resulting framework will be a step toward more reliable deployment of UAV swarms in GPS-denied or cluttered environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight coupling of estimation error bounds—derived directly from the properties of the relative measurement network—into the distributed control law synthesis, rather than treating the observer and controller as separate, cascaded modules. This moves beyond standard separation principles to a co-design that actively manages uncertainty.

proposals.title: Distributed Stability Analysis of Observer-Based Attitude Control for Multi-Agent Systems with Relative-Only Measurements
proposals.motivation: Distributed multi-agent systems, such as UAV swarms, increasingly rely on observer-based controllers for robust operation under sensor limitations and communication constraints. However, pre-2026 stability analyses often treat the observer and controller separately or assume full-state feedback, neglecting the coupled dynamics and network effects inherent in distributed relative sensing frameworks. A formal, integrated stability proof is critical for ensuring safety in applications like cooperative encirclement and collision-resilient flight.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing a Lyapunov function that jointly considers the estimation error dynamics from a distributed observer and the tracking error dynamics of a neural network-based antisynchronization controller, we can formally derive sufficient conditions for asymptotic stability of the overall networked system.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative distance-based positioning and neural antisynchronization control framework from Xie's prior work. The method involves: 1) Designing a distributed nonlinear observer for each agent to estimate neighboring states using only local relative measurements (e.g., from vision or UWB). 2) Integrating this observer's output into a neural adaptive attitude controller. 3) Employing a composite Lyapunov analysis and tools from input-to-state stability (ISS) to analyze the interconnected observer-controller-loop, deriving stability conditions in terms of network connectivity gains, and observer convergence rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous stability certificate for a class of distributed, measurement-constrained control systems, bridging a gap between theoretical multi-agent systems and practical robust control. It will yield explicit design guidelines for tuning observer and controller parameters to guarantee stable cooperative maneuvers, directly enhancing the reliability of algorithms for target encirclement and collision resilience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the unified stability analysis for a distributed neural control system fed by a relative-measurement-based observer, explicitly accounting for the error propagation between estimation and control in the network. This integrated approach is distinct from analyses that assume perfect state information or treat components in isolation.

proposals.title: Distributed Observer-Based Control for Multi-Agent Systems with Flexible Dynamics Using Relative Localization
proposals.motivation: Multi-agent systems for tasks like payload transport or cooperative manipulation increasingly involve agents with flexible appendages (e.g., booms, arms), introducing complex, distributed dynamics and unmodeled oscillations. Pre-2026 control methods often treat agents as rigid bodies or use simplified models, which can lead to performance degradation and instability when flexibility is significant. A framework that integrates high-fidelity flexible dynamics with distributed, measurement-based control is needed for robust cooperative behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a fully flexible finite-element boom model with a distributed, relative-measurement-based observer and controller will enable a team of agents to achieve precise formation control and task execution while actively suppressing detrimental structural vibrations.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation framework where each agent's flexible boom is modeled using a nonlinear finite-element method (pre-2026) to capture distributed flexibility and large deformations. A distributed nonlinear observer, using only local relative distance and bearing measurements (inspired by Xie's prior work on relative positioning), will estimate the full state of the flexible structure and neighboring agents. This estimate will feed into a neural network-based antisynchronization controller (extending Xie's neural control methods) to achieve the desired formation while damping oscillations through cooperative control actions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel simulation and control architecture that bridges high-fidelity flexible body dynamics with practical distributed sensing constraints. It will demonstrate a method for vibration-aware cooperative control, expanding the applicability of multi-agent systems to tasks involving structurally compliant elements. The results will offer design guidelines for integrating physical modeling with distributed estimation and control.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight integration of a high-fidelity, fully flexible distributed parameter system model with a distributed observer-controller that relies solely on local relative measurements, a combination not deeply explored in pre-2026 multi-agent literature. This moves beyond treating flexibility as a simple lumped disturbance.

proposals.title: Distributed Containment Control Under Unbounded Leader Dynamics via Relative Error and Neural Adaptive Compensation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 multi-agent consensus and containment control literature predominantly assumes the leader's control input is bounded or zero. This is restrictive for real-world scenarios where a leader may execute aggressive, open-loop maneuvers (e.g., evasive actions, trajectory tracking with unbounded reference accelerations). The stability and performance guarantees of existing distributed controllers, which rely on relative measurements and local interactions, break down when the leader's input grows unboundedly over time, posing a significant theoretical and practical gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a neural network-based adaptive estimator within a distributed relative-position-based control framework, follower agents can compensate for the destabilizing effects of an unbounded leader input, achieving practical containment or consensus with bounded tracking errors.
proposals.method: We will extend a relative distance-based positioning framework, similar to Xie's prior work. Each follower will use only local relative measurements from neighbors to estimate the combined effect of the leader's unknown, potentially unbounded input and nonlinear dynamics via a Radial Basis Function (RBF) neural network. This estimate will feed into a distributed adaptive backstepping controller. Stability will be analyzed using Lyapunov methods to prove uniformly ultimately bounded (UUB) errors for all followers, even as the leader's input grows polynomially or exponentially.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first distributed control solution with formal stability guarantees for containment/consensus with unbounded leader inputs. It will extend the applicability of multi-agent systems to high-dynamic scenarios. The integration of neural adaptive compensation within a relative-measurement-only framework offers a novel design paradigm for resilient cooperative control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly relaxing the universal bounded-input assumption for the leader in containment control and providing a provably stable solution using only local relative information, which is non-trivial as unbounded inputs can propagate through the network and cause divergence.

proposals.title: Distributed Robust Encirclement Control for Multi-Agent Systems via Relative Distance-Based Sliding Mode Control with Disturbance Observers
proposals.motivation: Multi-agent systems for target encirclement, as studied in Xie's prior work, are highly vulnerable to external disturbances (e.g., wind gusts) and model uncertainties, which can degrade performance and cause mission failure. Pre-2026 robust control techniques like H-infinity and sliding mode control are well-established for single systems but their integration into distributed, relative-measurement-only frameworks for cooperative control remains a significant challenge with high practical impact.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a distributed sliding mode controller with a finite-time disturbance observer into a relative distance-based positioning framework will guarantee robust encirclement of non-cooperative targets despite bounded external disturbances and inter-agent collision risks.
proposals.method: The method extends the relative distance-based positioning scheme from Xie's prior work. A novel distributed integral sliding mode surface will be designed using only relative position and velocity measurements between neighboring agents. A finite-time disturbance observer will be embedded in each agent's local controller to estimate and compensate for matched disturbances. The control law will be synthesized using Lyapunov methods to prove stability and enforce collision avoidance constraints derived from the 'Collision Resilience Behaviors' paper, ensuring robust encirclement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally verified distributed control architecture that guarantees robust performance for multi-agent encirclement under disturbances. It will bridge the gap between decentralized relative sensing frameworks and advanced robust control theory, offering a template for hardening distributed coordination tasks against real-world uncertainties.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of a finite-time disturbance observer with a distributed sliding mode controller that operates exclusively on local relative measurements, a combination not fully explored in pre-2026 literature for the specific problem of robust target encirclement with collision constraints.

proposals.title: Distributed Adaptive Control for Non-Interlaced Nonlinear Multi-Agent Systems via Relative Measurement and Neural Approximation
proposals.motivation: A significant limitation in distributed nonlinear control, such as backstepping, is the reliance on systems being transformable into a strict lower-triangular (interlaced) form. Many practical multi-agent systems, like heterogeneous UAV swarms with complex dynamics or collision resilience behaviors, do not possess this structure, precluding the application of powerful existing theoretical tools. This gap restricts the deployment of robust distributed control for a wide class of uncertain, cooperative, or noncooperative target engagement scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed control framework, combining relative state/error measurements with adaptive neural network approximators, can stabilize and enforce performance for uncertain nonlinear multi-agent systems that violate the interlaced form requirement, without relying on full-state linearization or centralized information.
proposals.method: The approach will extend the researcher's prior work on relative distance-based positioning and neural antisynchronization control. We will develop a two-layer architecture: 1) A distributed estimation layer using relative measurements (e.g., relative distance, bearing) to reconstruct locally unavailable states or error signals, and 2) A local adaptive controller employing Radial Basis Function (RBF) neural networks to compensate for the uncertain, non-interlaced nonlinearities in each agent's dynamics. Stability will be proven via Lyapunov analysis for the interconnected system, ensuring boundedness of all signals and convergence of the cooperative task error.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control design template for a broader class of nonlinear multi-agent systems, directly relaxing a major structural assumption. It will contribute a theoretically grounded, distributed framework matching practical system constraints, enhancing the applicability of advanced control to real-world robotic networks with complex, uncertain dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated use of distributed relative measurement filters with local neural approximators specifically to bypass the interlaced form requirement, a combination not systematically explored in pre-2026 literature for multi-agent nonlinear control under uncertainty.

proposals.title: Distributed Adaptive Backstepping Control for Multi-Agent Encirclement with Online Uncertainty Estimation
proposals.motivation: Distributed control of multi-agent systems for tasks like target encirclement, as seen in Dr. Xie's prior work, is critically challenged by unknown or time-varying agent dynamics and environmental disturbances. Pre-2026 methods often rely on robust controllers or neural networks, which can be conservative or lack formal guarantees for parameter convergence. An adaptive control framework that explicitly estimates uncertainties online within a distributed structure is needed to enhance precision and robustness in cooperative missions.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating adaptive parameter estimation laws with an extended backstepping control design, formulated using relative distance and local measurement graphs, will enable a distributed multi-agent system to achieve precise formation control while simultaneously identifying and compensating for model uncertainties and external disturbances.
proposals.method: We will develop a distributed control law based on an extended backstepping procedure for a network of agents with second-order dynamics. Adaptive update laws will be embedded at each recursive step to estimate unknown parameters (e.g., drag coefficients) and lumped disturbances online, using only relative position/velocity measurements from a local sensing graph. The Lyapunov stability analysis will guarantee uniformly ultimately bounded tracking errors for the encirclement task and boundedness of all parameter estimates. The controller will be validated in simulation for a quadrotor swarm scenario with aerodynamic uncertainties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally stable, distributed adaptive control architecture for multi-agent systems that explicitly decouples uncertainty estimation from the cooperative control objective. It will extend the applicability of backstepping to networked systems with limited sensing, contributing a new integration of adaptive control with graph-based distributed frameworks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of distributed, relative-measurement-based backstepping with online parameter estimation specifically for formation/encirclement tasks, moving beyond centralized adaptive schemes or disturbance observers that do not provide direct parameter identification in a networked context.

proposals.title: A Relative-Measurement-Based Robust Controller for SISO Non-Minimum-Phase Systems with Parametric Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Non-minimum-phase (NMP) systems, characterized by unstable zero dynamics, present a fundamental challenge in control design, especially under parameter uncertainty. While advanced multi-input control strategies exist, a simpler, robust single-input single-output (SISO) controller is highly desirable for applications with limited sensing/actuation, such as low-cost UAVs or networked agents where distributed, local control is paramount. This work addresses the gap between complex multi-agent control frameworks and the need for reliable, simplified controllers for individual uncertain NMP agents.
proposals.hypothesis: A SISO controller, synthesized using only relative output measurements and explicitly accounting for parametric uncertainty, can stabilize a class of NMP systems where traditional robust designs (e.g., standard H-infinity) fail due to the interaction of NMP zeros and model errors.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'relative distance-based positioning' concept from multi-agent control to define a virtual reference for the SISO system. A robust controller will be designed using a loop-shaping approach within the Quantitative Feedback Theory (QFT) framework, which handles parametric uncertainty explicitly. The control law will be implemented using only local, relative measurements of the output error, akin to the 'local measurements' theme in distributed frameworks. Stability and performance will be analyzed using the Small-Gain Theorem and simulated on a canonical NMP system (e.g., a simplified quadrotor pitch dynamics model with an aft-mounted sensor).
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel, simpler design pathway for robust control of uncertain SISO NMP systems, directly applicable to individual agents in a distributed network. It will formally bridge concepts from distributed relative-measurement-based control to the classical problem of SISO robust stabilization, offering a new perspective on handling NMP constraints with uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of the 'relative' measurement paradigm from distributed multi-agent control with the explicit uncertainty-handling of QFT, applied specifically to the difficult SISO NMP problem. This creates a controller that is both structurally simple and robust, a combination not typically found in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Distributed H∞ System Identification for Multi-Agent Formations with Relative Measurements
proposals.motivation: Robust control of multi-agent systems, such as UAV swarms for encirclement or resilient formation control, requires accurate models. Pre-2026, system identification often uses least-squares criteria, which are sensitive to worst-case disturbances like adversarial noise or unmodeled couplings. A distributed identification method with a guaranteed H∞ error bound is crucial for ensuring the subsequent robust controller's performance in safety-critical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: The parameters of an interlaced-form multi-agent system can be identified from local input-output and relative measurement data via a distributed optimization framework that explicitly minimizes the worst-case ℓ2-gain of the estimation error, leading to models that are robust to bounded disturbances and suitable for H∞ control synthesis.
proposals.method: We will formulate the identification as a convex optimization problem minimizing an H∞ norm criterion, building on pre-2026 robust control and LMIs. The 'interlaced-form' system structure, inspired by Xie's prior work on distributed frameworks, will be exploited to decentralize the computation. Each agent will solve a local sub-problem using only its own input-output data and relative state measurements from neighbors, coordinating via an ADMM-based protocol to converge to the global H∞-optimal model parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first distributed algorithm for H∞-norm system identification, directly producing models with certified worst-case error bounds. It will enable the synthesis of provably robust distributed controllers for multi-agent tasks, bridging a gap between robust identification and distributed control theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the H∞ robustness criterion from centralized control with a distributed, relative-measurement-based computational architecture tailored for multi-agent systems, an approach not addressed in pre-2026 identification literature which is predominantly centralized or uses stochastic error measures.

proposals.title: Distributed Formation Tracking via Relative-State Consensus and Local Trajectory Estimation
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent systems, reliance on a global leader or a shared reference state is a single point of failure and requires extensive communication infrastructure. Applications like UAV swarm surveillance or multi-robot exploration demand robust, decentralized tracking where the desired trajectory is not broadcast but emerges from local interactions. Pre-2026 work on distributed control often assumes agents have access to the global target state or its derivatives, limiting applicability in communication-denied or adversarial environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A formation of agents can track a dynamic desired trajectory with bounded error without any agent possessing full global knowledge, by fusing local estimates of the target state derived from relative measurements with neighboring agents through a consensus protocol.
proposals.method: We propose a two-layer distributed architecture. First, each agent runs a local estimator (e.g., a distributed Kalman filter) to reconstruct an approximation of the global target state using only relative position measurements to neighbors and locally sensed target information (e.g., bearing). Second, these local estimates are fused via a dynamic consensus algorithm to achieve agreement on a virtual leader state. A relative distance-based formation controller, inspired by Xie's prior work on neural antisynchronization, will then use this agreed-upon virtual state and inter-agent relative positions to achieve and maintain the tracking formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous framework for leaderless formation tracking with formal stability and convergence guarantees under communication constraints. It will extend distributed control theory by decoupling global trajectory knowledge from individual agent capabilities, enhancing system resilience. The proposed method will be validated in simulation for quadrotor swarms, demonstrating collision-resilient behaviors during tracking maneuvers.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior distributed tracking that often assumes a subset of agents knows the global trajectory, our method ensures no single agent has complete knowledge; the target state is collectively estimated and agreed upon. This integrates distributed estimation and control in a novel way for this specific problem, moving beyond static formation consensus to dynamic trajectory tracking.

proposals.title: Distributed Multi-Agent Encirclement via Adaptive-Gain Sliding Mode Control with Relative Distance Measurements
proposals.motivation: Distributed multi-agent control for tasks like target encirclement, as studied by the researcher, is critically vulnerable to unknown, bounded disturbances (e.g., wind gusts, model uncertainties). Conventional sliding-mode control (SMC) requires a priori knowledge of disturbance bounds to set fixed gains, leading to overly conservative, high-gain controllers that induce chattering and excessive energy use if bounds are overestimated. This limits practical deployment in resilient UAV systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating an adaptive law to tune SMC gains online, based on the real-time evolution of a sliding surface, will enable a distributed multi-agent formation to maintain precise relative positioning for encirclement despite disturbances with completely unknown bounds, while simultaneously minimizing control chattering.
proposals.method: We will extend the researcher's prior relative distance-based positioning framework. Each agent will implement a local adaptive SMC law where the switching gain is dynamically adjusted via an adaptation rule, such as σ̇ = κ|s| with σ as the adaptive gain, s as the sliding variable, and κ > 0. The control will use only local relative measurements from neighboring agents, ensuring a distributed architecture. Stability and finite-time convergence will be proven using Lyapunov analysis, combining techniques from adaptive control and the researcher's work on neural antisynchronization for robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally stable, distributed control protocol that guarantees encirclement performance without prior disturbance bound knowledge, directly enhancing collision resilience in UAV swarms. It will contribute a new integration of adaptive control principles into relative-measurement-based multi-agent frameworks, reducing conservatism and energy use.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the specific relative-distance-based encirclement problem with gain-adaptive SMC in a strictly distributed setting, where each agent adapts based only on local information, avoiding the need for a centralized disturbance estimator or bound assumption.

proposals.title: Distributed Multi-Agent Consensus with Prescribed Transient Performance via Relative Error Transformation and Neural Network Compensation
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent systems for target encirclement or distributed formation, consensus errors must converge within strict, user-defined transient bounds (overshoot, convergence rate, steady-state error) to ensure safety and performance, especially under sensor limitations and disturbances. Existing distributed controllers often guarantee asymptotic stability but lack explicit, enforceable transient performance guarantees, which is critical for real-world UAV applications.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a distributed prescribed performance control (PPC) framework with a neural network-based disturbance observer, the transient and steady-state behavior of consensus errors in a network of agents using only relative local measurements can be rigorously bounded according to pre-specified performance functions.
proposals.method: We will design a distributed control law where each agent's consensus error, derived from relative distance-based positioning, is transformed via a performance function and a smooth error mapping. This transformed error will drive a nominal consensus protocol. To handle model uncertainties and external disturbances, a radial basis function neural network (RBFNN) will be employed as a local adaptive compensator for each agent, using only locally available relative state information. Stability will be proven via Lyapunov analysis, ensuring all signals remain bounded and the original consensus error remains within the prescribed performance bounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous distributed control framework that guarantees predefined transient performance for multi-agent consensus, extending beyond asymptotic results. It will demonstrate a novel integration of PPC with neural network compensation in a fully distributed setting, validated through simulation on a quadrotor encirclement scenario.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of prescribed performance control—typically applied in centralized or single-agent systems—with a fully distributed, relative-measurement-only architecture for multi-agent consensus, augmented by local neural networks for robustness, a combination not deeply explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Inverse Graph Design for Prescribed-Time Consensus in Multi-Agent Systems via Relative Measurement-Based Optimization
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent control, achieving consensus within a user-defined, finite time is critical for time-sensitive applications like UAV swarm rendezvous or distributed sensor fusion. Existing work primarily analyzes convergence given a fixed network, but the inverse problem—designing the network to guarantee a desired convergence time—remains largely open. Solving this would provide a powerful system-level design tool, moving from analysis to synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: For a class of prescribed-time consensus protocols using relative state measurements, there exists a computationally tractable method to synthesize communication graph topologies or edge weights that guarantee convergence within any arbitrarily specified, finite time bound.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a constrained optimization over the graph Laplacian matrix. Building on the researcher's expertise in relative positioning and distributed control, we will derive an analytical link between the algebraic connectivity of the graph and the convergence rate of a prescribed-time consensus protocol. The core algorithm will involve solving a semi-definite program (SDP) to find feasible Laplacian matrices that satisfy spectral constraints for the desired convergence time, potentially incorporating sparsity penalties to promote simpler, local communication structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for network co-design in multi-agent systems, where control performance (convergence time) directly informs communication requirements. It will extend distributed control theory from analysis to synthesis for temporal specifications. The resulting algorithms will be validated in simulation for agent encirclement and formation scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While graph design for asymptotic convergence is studied, the explicit, inverse synthesis of networks for *prescribed* finite-time convergence, directly from a temporal specification, is a novel and non-trivial extension. It combines prescribed-time control theory with graph spectral optimization in a new way.

proposals.title: Finite-Time Dispersion Control for Multi-Agent Systems via Modified Error Functions and Relative Distance-Based Positioning
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent robotics, achieving rapid and precise spatial dispersion is critical for applications like area coverage, search and rescue, and sensor deployment. Pre-2026 control methods often achieve asymptotic convergence, which is too slow for dynamic environments, or rely on global information, limiting scalability. A distributed, finite-time solution using only local relative measurements would significantly enhance operational speed and robustness in decentralized networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing a novel, modified error function that encodes inter-agent separation constraints and integrating it with a distributed finite-time controller, a team of agents can achieve guaranteed dispersion to a specified minimum distance within a finite settling time using only relative positioning.
proposals.method: The method builds on the researcher's prior work on relative distance-based positioning and neural antisynchronization control. First, a modified error function will be constructed, incorporating a sigmoidal or power-term barrier to encode the finite-time separation objective. Second, a distributed finite-time consensus controller, potentially based on terminal sliding mode or homogeneous feedback techniques, will be synthesized using only local relative measurements (e.g., from UWB or vision). Stability and finite-time convergence will be proven via Lyapunov analysis and homogeneity theory. The framework will be validated in simulation and on quadrotor platforms, testing collision resilience during the dispersion maneuver.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new distributed control framework for finite-time dispersion, rigorously proving convergence. It will extend the theory of finite-time multi-agent coordination to separation tasks using only local sensing. The practical contribution is a robust, scalable algorithm for rapid deployment of agent swarms in GPS-denied environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of a modified error function specifically tailored for finite-time separation, combined with a distributed, relative-measurement-only controller. This moves beyond asymptotic encirclement or containment control to provide guaranteed, fast dispersion, a less explored dual problem in finite-time multi-agent systems.

proposals.title: Distributed Stochastic Synchronization Control for Multi-Agent Systems with Relative Measurements and Communication Noise
proposals.motivation: Multi-agent systems for tasks like target encirclement and formation flight rely on robust synchronization, but real-world deployments face stochastic disturbances in communication and relative sensing. Pre-2026 control designs often treat stability deterministically or assume perfect information, limiting their applicability to agents operating in noisy, uncertain environments where only local, relative measurements are available.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating derived stochastic Lyapunov stability criteria directly into the controller synthesis, we can design distributed control laws that guarantee synchronization with a quantifiable probability, even under persistent measurement and communication noise.
proposals.method: We will first derive sufficient conditions for mean-square bounded synchronization using a stochastic Lyapunov approach for a network of agents with dynamics akin to double integrators. These criteria, expressed as linear matrix inequalities (LMIs), will then be used to synthesize a distributed control law.
