The controller will utilize only relative position/velocity measurements from onboard sensors (e.g., vision, UWB) and noisy neighbor state estimates, employing a consensus-based update rule resilient to stochastic errors. The scheme will be validated in simulation and on a quadrotor testbed with induced communication packet loss and sensor noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, stability-guaranteed design framework for synchronization controllers in stochastic settings, moving beyond ad-hoc robustness checks. It will deliver a concrete, implementable control algorithm for resilient multi-agent coordination, directly applicable to the researcher's prior work on encirclement and collision resilience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct co-design of stochastic stability analysis and distributed controller synthesis for synchronization, using relative measurements under noise, rather than applying analysis post-hoc to an existing deterministic controller.

proposals.title: Distributed Resilient Encirclement Control for Internet-of-Things Swarms Using Relative Distance-Based Positioning
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of Internet-of-Things (IoT) and wireless sensor networks for tasks like environmental monitoring and infrastructure surveillance requires robust, distributed control algorithms that function under real-world constraints. Pre-2026, these networked systems face challenges like communication delays, packet loss, and dynamic topology, which can destabilize theoretical multi-agent coordination schemes. Validating advanced distributed control frameworks on realistic network models is a critical step toward deployable autonomy.
proposals.hypothesis: The relative distance-based positioning and neural antisynchronization control framework, previously developed for target encirclement, can be adapted to provide resilient formation and coverage behaviors in simulated IoT sensor networks experiencing non-ideal, Internet-like communication links.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core 'relative distance-based positioning' algorithm from Xie's prior work to operate on a graph model of an IoT swarm, where agents have only local, noisy range measurements. The neural antisynchronization controller will be modified to incorporate communication latency and dropout models derived from real-world IoT protocols (e.g., MQTT, CoAP). Simulation will be conducted in a ROS/Gazebo environment with the NS-3 network simulator integrated to provide high-fidelity, stochastic network condition emulation for performance evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first rigorous performance analyses of a distributed encirclement/coverage control algorithm under realistic Internet-based network impairments. It will yield concrete design guidelines for tuning such controllers for resilience and establish a benchmark simulation methodology for networked multi-agent systems.
proposals.novelty: While distributed control is well-studied in theory, its application to IoT swarms with explicit, high-fidelity models of Internet protocol stack behavior (not just simple graph theories) is novel. The integration of a neural antisynchronization controller with network simulator-in-the-loop testing for resilience validation is a non-obvious and necessary advancement.

proposals.title: Robust Synchronization of Multi-Agent Systems via Distributionally Robust Optimization with Relative Measurements
proposals.motivation: Distributed control of multi-agent systems for tasks like encirclement or formation often relies on relative measurements (e.g., distance, bearing) which are subject to stochastic noise, delays, and packet loss. Pre-2026 methods, including neural synchronization controllers, often treat uncertainties in an ad-hoc manner, lacking formal performance guarantees under worst-case disturbances within a probabilistic family. This limits the reliability of UAV swarms and mobile sensor networks in real-world, uncertain environments.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating multi-agent synchronization as a distributionally robust optimization (DRO) problem over an ambiguity set of uncertainty distributions, we can synthesize controllers that guarantee robust performance and probabilistic constraint satisfaction using only noisy relative local measurements.
proposals.method: We will extend a relative distance-based positioning framework to a DRO formulation. The ambiguity set will be defined using Wasserstein metrics around an empirical distribution of historical measurement errors. A distributed, saddle-point algorithm will be developed to solve the resulting min-max optimization problem, where the inner max evaluates worst-case expected cost over the ambiguity set and the outer min designs local control policies. This will integrate with a distributed estimation layer for relative state localization under the same uncertainty model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, optimization-theoretic foundation for robust synchronization with verifiable probabilistic certificates. It will yield a novel distributed control algorithm that is less conservative than standard robust control yet more reliable than stochastic optimization assuming a known distribution. The framework will be validated in simulation for the multi-target encirclement problem under stochastic sensing faults.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of distributionally robust optimization from operations research with distributed relative-measurement-based control for multi-agent systems, moving beyond deterministic robust control or stochastic control with perfectly known distributions.

proposals.title: Distributed Leader-Follower Consensus with Stochastic Disturbances: A Relative Measurement-Based Control Framework
proposals.motivation: Leader-follower consensus is a fundamental building block for multi-agent systems, with applications in formation control and distributed sensing. However, existing control strategies often assume perfect communication or deterministic dynamics, which is unrealistic in real-world deployments with noisy sensors and environmental disturbances. A rigorous analysis of a minimal two-agent system under stochastic noise is needed to establish fundamental performance limits and robust design principles.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed control law, synthesized using only relative state measurements and explicitly accounting for stochastic disturbances, can guarantee mean-square bounded consensus error for a leader-follower pair, and the error bound can be analytically characterized as a function of noise intensity and controller gains.
proposals.method: We will adapt the relative distance-based positioning and neural antisynchronization control framework from Xie's prior work to a simplified two-agent linear dynamical system. The follower's controller will be designed using only noisy relative position/velocity measurements. We will model disturbances as additive white Gaussian noise and employ stochastic Lyapunov analysis to prove stability in the mean-square sense. The control gains will be optimized using a Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI) approach to minimize the proven consensus error bound.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally verified, stochastic stability guarantee for a core distributed control topology, directly extending deterministic results. It will yield explicit, tunable performance bounds for consensus error, offering a principled design tool for robust controller synthesis in noisy environments. The simplified setup serves as a critical benchmark for scaling the framework to larger networks.
proposals.novelty: While leader-follower consensus is well-studied deterministically, the explicit integration of stochastic disturbance analysis with a relative-measurement-only controller derived from Xie's neural antisynchronization framework is novel. The focus on deriving an *analytical* performance bound, rather than just demonstrating stability, provides a stronger and more practical design insight.

proposals.title: Distributed Dual Desynchronization Control for Multi-Agent Systems via Relative Measurement-Based Neural Controllers
proposals.motivation: While extensive research pre-2026 focuses on achieving and maintaining synchronization in multi-agent systems, controlled desynchronization is a critical, understudied problem. It is essential for applications like dispersing swarms for area coverage, avoiding resource conflicts in networked systems, or breaking undesirable lock-step behaviors in biological or robotic networks under stochastic disturbances. This work addresses the deliberate induction of stable, predictable asynchronous patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed control framework, leveraging only relative local measurements, can be designed to actively drive a network of agents into a dual (or multi-cluster) desynchronized state, even in the presence of similar stochastic inputs that typically promote synchronization.
proposals.method: We will extend the researcher's prior work on relative distance-based positioning and neural antisynchronization control. The core method involves designing a novel distributed error dynamics controller for each agent. This controller will integrate a repulsive potential field based on relative state measurements (e.g., relative distance, bearing) with a purposefully designed neural oscillator whose phase is tuned to oppose synchronization with neighbors. Stability and convergence to a desynchronized pattern will be analyzed using Lyapunov methods and graph theory, validated in simulation for quadrotor-like dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a formal framework and provably stable controller designs for the active desynchronization problem. It will expand the theory of multi-agent coordination beyond consensus and synchronization, providing tools for generating diverse spatiotemporal patterns. The results will be directly applicable to enhancing collision resilience and operational flexibility in UAV swarms.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formulating and solving the 'dual desynchronization' as a distributed control objective, deliberately exploiting stochastic conditions to achieve separation, which is the inverse of the typical robust synchronization problem. This contrasts with most pre-2026 work where desynchronization is an unwanted failure mode to be corrected.

proposals.title: Distributed Control of Multi-Agent Systems under Joint Quantization and Delays: A Delay-Dependent Sector-Bounded Operator Approach
proposals.motivation: The deployment of distributed control for networked agents, such as UAV swarms, is fundamentally challenged by simultaneous communication constraints: quantization for bandwidth efficiency and time-varying network delays. Pre-2026 literature typically treats these effects separately, leading to overly conservative stability conditions and suboptimal controller-quantizer co-design. A unified analysis is critical for enabling robust, high-performance coordination in bandwidth-limited, adversarial, or congested network environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling the combined quantization-delay effect as a single nonlinear operator with delay-dependent sector bounds will yield less conservative stability criteria than existing additive disturbance models. This framework will enable the joint synthesis of distributed controllers and dynamic quantizers that are provably robust to the coupled nonlinear dynamics introduced by networked delays.
proposals.method: We will model the joint quantization-delay effect as a nonlinear operator whose sector bounds are explicit functions of the current and past delay values. A novel Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional will be constructed, incorporating cross-terms between the delayed state and the quantization error to capture their interdependence. Stability analysis will employ linear matrix inequalities (LMIs) derived from this functional, leading to delay-dependent stability conditions. The method will be validated in simulation for a multi-agent encirclement or formation control task, building on the researcher's prior work in relative positioning and neural antisynchronization control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, less conservative analytical tool for networked control systems subject to simultaneous non-idealities. It will deliver co-design guidelines for quantizer and controller parameters that explicitly account for delay-quantization coupling. The results will advance the theoretical foundation for resilient distributed control of agent networks operating under stringent communication constraints.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the unified 'joint operator' perspective and the derivation of delay-dependent sector conditions for the coupled effect, moving beyond the standard approach of treating quantization and delay as independent additive disturbances. This integrated modeling directly addresses the nonlinear interaction that existing methods overlook.

proposals.title: Distributed Super-Twisting Control with Adaptive Dynamic Quantization for Multi-Agent Systems under Communication Constraints
proposals.motivation: In networked multi-agent systems, communication bandwidth is limited, necessitating signal quantization. However, standard sliding mode control (SMC) under quantized feedback suffers from severe chattering and energy waste, degrading performance and causing actuator wear. While the super-twisting algorithm (STA) reduces chattering, its integration with adaptive quantization to handle distributed, relative measurements remains an open challenge for robust multi-agent control.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a distributed, second-order super-twisting controller with an online adaptive quantization strategy, where resolution and controller gains are co-adjusted based on local and neighboring state errors, will guarantee practical finite-time consensus while suppressing chattering more effectively than static or non-adaptive quantized SMC.
proposals.method: We will develop a distributed control framework where each agent uses only relative distance-based measurements from local sensors or neighbors. A super-twisting sliding manifold will be designed for the consensus error dynamics. A dynamic quantizer with adjustable resolution will be embedded in the feedback loop. An adaptation law, inspired by tuning functions, will jointly adjust the quantizer's parameters and the STA's boundary layer gains based on a real-time estimate of the quantization error magnitude and the sliding variable. Stability will be proven using Lyapunov methods for discontinuous systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, provably stable control framework for multi-agent systems operating under strict bandwidth limits, directly applicable to UAV encirclement and formation tasks. It will advance the field by rigorously unifying adaptive quantization with high-order SMC, offering a practical solution to the chattering problem in networked control.
proposals.novelty: The synergistic, online co-adaptation of the quantizer and the super-twisting controller parameters based on distributed error signals is a novel departure from methods that treat quantization as a fixed disturbance or adapt only the controller.

proposals.title: Distributed Event-Triggered Control with Network-Adaptive Predictive Quantization for Multi-Agent Systems
proposals.motivation: Distributed control of multi-agent systems over wireless networks is crucial for applications like drone swarms and sensor networks. However, naive combinations of event-triggered control (to save energy) and communication quantization (to save bandwidth) can destabilize systems under realistic network imperfections like packet loss and variable delays. A co-design framework that dynamically adapts to network conditions is needed to ensure robustness while conserving resources.
proposals.hypothesis: A co-designed control framework, where the event-triggering condition and the codebook of a predictive quantizer are jointly adapted using online estimates of network state (e.g., delay, loss), will maintain Lyapunov stability and performance for distributed agents under imperfect networks while significantly reducing communication and energy use compared to static or decoupled designs.
proposals.method: We will extend a relative distance-based positioning framework with a distributed estimator for local network state (delay, loss probability). This estimate will dynamically parameterize a local event-triggering rule and a predictive logarithmic quantizer. The quantizer will encode not just the current state but a prediction of its likely evolution over the estimated delay horizon. Stability will be proven using a Lyapunov function incorporating quantization error and network disturbance bounds, validated in simulation for a quadrotor encirclement task.
proposals.contribution: 1) A provably stable, distributed control scheme that jointly optimizes event-triggering and quantization. 2) A novel predictive quantization method that compensates for packet loss/delay using network state estimates. 3) Demonstrated resource savings (bandwidth, energy) for multi-agent tasks under realistic network models, advancing the practical deployment of distributed control systems.
proposals.novelty: The tight co-design of the event trigger and quantizer codebook based on real-time network estimation is novel, as is the use of predictive quantization to actively compensate for network imperfections rather than merely tolerate them.

proposals.title: Distributed Adaptive Control for Non-Strict-Feedback Multi-Agent Systems via Relative State Feedback and Neural Network Compensation
proposals.motivation: The strict-feedback form is a cornerstone of backstepping control design for nonlinear systems, but many practical multi-agent systems, such as quadrotors with complex aerodynamic coupling or agents with non-triangular actuation dynamics, do not conform to this structure. This limitation restricts the application of robust and adaptive control techniques to a narrower class of problems than encountered in real-world robotics and distributed systems. Developing methods that relax this requirement is crucial for advancing the control of complex, interconnected agents.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed control framework combining relative state feedback, neural network-based uncertainty approximators, and a novel error transformation can achieve stable cooperative objectives for a class of nonlinear multi-agent systems that are not transformable into a strict-feedback form.
proposals.method: We will leverage the researcher's expertise in relative measurements and neural antisynchronization to design a controller that uses only relative position/velocity information from neighboring agents. A key innovation will be the design of a distributed, filtered error signal that bypasses the need for recursive backstepping. Radial Basis Function (RBF) neural networks will be embedded in a decentralized manner to compensate for unknown nonlinearities and inter-agent coupling effects that violate the strict-feedback structure. Stability will be proven via Lyapunov analysis of the overall networked error dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical framework and a practical controller design for cooperative control of a broader class of nonlinear multi-agent systems. It will directly extend the applicability of adaptive neural control beyond the strict-feedback paradigm. The resulting method will be validated in simulation for the encirclement of noncooperative targets using agent models with non-triangular nonlinearities.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of a non-recursive, relative-information-based control law with neural compensation specifically tailored for systems lacking a strict-feedback structure, moving beyond the standard backstepping approach prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Distributed Encirclement Control for Multi-Agent Systems with Unknown Control Directions via Relative Measurement-Based Nussbaum Protocols
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent control tasks like target encirclement, agents often have uncertain, nonlinear dynamics where the sign of control effectiveness (control direction) is unknown, precluding standard feedback designs. Pre-2026, distributed solutions using relative measurements (e.g., distances, bearings) for such problems are scarce, limiting robust deployment of UAV swarms or robotic teams in unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating Nussbaum-type adaptive gains with a distributed, relative-measurement-based control framework, a team of agents can achieve stable cooperative objectives like encirclement or formation, even when individual agents have completely unknown control directions and nonlinear actuator characteristics.
proposals.method: We will extend the researcher's prior work on relative distance-based positioning and distributed frameworks. The core method is a novel distributed controller where each agent employs a local Nussbaum gain (e.g., Nussbaum function N(ζ)=ζ²cos(ζ)) to estimate its unknown control direction online. This gain will modulate a baseline control law derived solely from relative state measurements (e.g., distances to neighbors/targets). Stability will be proven via Lyapunov analysis for the networked error dynamics, ensuring all signals remain bounded while the cooperative task is achieved.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first distributed control protocol that guarantees encirclement/formation stability under simultaneous unknown control directions and relative-information constraints. It will extend the applicability of Nussbaum gain techniques from single-agent to networked systems, offering a practical design for resilient multi-robot systems with severe actuator uncertainties.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of the distributed, relative-measurement-only paradigm (key to the researcher's prior work) with the challenge of unknown control directions, creating a non-trivial synthesis where local Nussbaum adaptations must remain consistent across the network to achieve global cooperation.

proposals.title: Distributed Output-Feedback Encirclement Control via Relative Localization and Neural Observers
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent encirclement and formation control, the common assumption of full-state feedback (e.g., precise velocity and attitude measurements) is often impractical for cost-effective or sensor-limited platforms like quadrotors. Pre-2026 work, including the researcher's own, heavily relies on state measurements for neural synchronization and relative positioning controllers. Removing this assumption is crucial for real-world robustness and deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed control framework combining a nonlinear state observer with a relative-output-based neural controller can achieve stable multi-target encirclement using only local, relative distance and bearing measurements, eliminating the need for agents' own full state feedback.
proposals.method: We will extend the researcher's prior relative distance-based positioning framework. First, a distributed nonlinear observer (e.g., a high-gain or sliding-mode observer) will be designed to estimate the unmeasured states (like linear velocities) using only onboard relative measurements (e.g., from UWB or vision). This estimated state will then feed a modified neural anti-synchronization controller, inspired by the researcher's prior work, to generate control inputs. Stability will be proven using Lyapunov and input-to-state stability arguments for the interconnected observer-controller system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally verified output-feedback control law for distributed encirclement, directly enhancing the applicability of the researcher's collision resilience and framework matching concepts. It will contribute a new design paradigm at the intersection of distributed nonlinear observation and neural network-based control for multi-agent systems.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a rigorous nonlinear observer design specifically with a neural anti-synchronization control law for relative-positioning-based tasks is novel. Unlike standard output-feedback methods, this approach is tailored to the unique challenges of distributed encirclement where the reference (target motion) is also estimated from relative measurements.

proposals.title: Distributed Neural Control for Multi-Agent Systems with Uncertain Non-Strict-Feedback Nonlinearities
proposals.motivation: Distributed control for multi-agent systems (MAS) has predominantly focused on strict-feedback or feedback-linearizable forms, which limits applicability to complex agents like agile UAVs with non-affine control inputs or internal dynamics. Extending these methods to pure-feedback and other non-strict-feedback structures is crucial for real-world deployment where agent models are highly uncertain and do not conform to simplified forms. This gap hinders the robust application of distributed frameworks to advanced cooperative tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a novel neural network-based observer with a distributed command-filtered backstepping design, it is possible to achieve stable cooperative control for a network of uncertain nonlinear agents in pure-feedback form. The proposed method will ensure bounded tracking errors and maintain network connectivity using only relative local measurements.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative distance-based positioning framework to a pure-feedback structure where the control input does not appear linearly. The core technical approach is a two-step design: first, a distributed neural network observer will estimate the unmeasured states and compounded uncertainties for each agent using local relative measurements. Second, a command-filtered recursive backstepping procedure, augmented with a neural antisynchronization term akin to prior work, will generate virtual and actual control laws to circumvent the algebraic loop problem inherent in non-strict-feedback forms. Stability will be proven via Lyapunov analysis for the entire networked system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new distributed control framework applicable to a broader class of nonlinear multi-agent systems, specifically those in pure-feedback form. It will contribute a rigorous stability proof for the networked case and demonstrate the method's efficacy in simulation for a cooperative encirclement task with non-affine quadrotor dynamics, advancing the field beyond strict-feedback assumptions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of a distributed neural observer with command-filtered backstepping specifically tailored for networked pure-feedback systems, a significant and non-trivial extension of the researcher's prior strict-feedback and relative positioning work. This addresses the core algebraic loop challenge in a multi-agent context using only local information.

proposals.title: Distributed Robust Adaptive Control with Output Saturation for Multi-Agent Encirclement under Relative Measurements
proposals.motivation: In safety-critical multi-agent applications like UAV-based target encirclement, actuator saturation and physical state constraints are ubiquitous. Pre-2026 robust adaptive controllers often treat these constraints as secondary, risking performance degradation or instability when limits are reached. A unified design that explicitly enforces constraints within the distributed, measurement-based control framework is essential for reliable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a smooth, time-varying barrier Lyapunov function (BLF) with a distributed robust adaptive controller, based on relative distance and bearing measurements, will guarantee prescribed performance and asymptotic convergence for multi-agent encirclement tasks while strictly enforcing output constraints.
proposals.method: We will extend Xie's prior relative positioning and neural antisynchronization control framework. The core is a novel adaptive controller using a BLF to encode output constraints (e.g., minimum safe distance, maximum control effort). The update law for the neural network approximator will be modified via a projection operator to ensure parameter boundedness under saturation. Stability will be proven using Lyapunov theory for the networked system, ensuring all signals remain bounded and the encirclement error converges within the constrained output space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally verified control design that bridges distributed adaptive control with hard safety constraints, a gap in pre-2026 literature. It will deliver a practical control scheme for resilient multi-robot systems where safety and performance must be co-guaranteed using only local relative measurements.
proposals.novelty: The integration of BLF-based constraint handling into a distributed, relative-measurement-only adaptive control architecture for multi-agent encirclement is novel. Unlike centralized or decoupled approaches, the constraint enforcement is embedded directly into the distributed adaptive law, ensuring network-wide safety.

proposals.title: Distributed Prescribed Performance Control for Multi-Agent Encirclement with Relative Measurements and Neural Network Compensation
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent encirclement and containment tasks, such as those for UAVs, existing distributed controllers often suffer from slow convergence and large transient overshoots, which can lead to collisions or mission failure under dynamic uncertainties. Pre-2026 prescribed performance control (PPC) and finite-time stability theory offer frameworks for guaranteeing transient bounds, but their integration into distributed, relative-measurement-based frameworks for nonlinear multi-agent systems remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a transformed error function from PPC with a distributed neural network-based estimator will enable a team of agents using only relative distance and bearing measurements to achieve encirclement of a target with user-prescribed transient performance (convergence rate, maximum overshoot) and finite-time convergence, despite model uncertainties and external disturbances.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative distance-based positioning framework from Xie's prior work. A prescribed performance function will bound the relative positioning errors for each agent. A distributed, local control law will be synthesized using the transformed error from the PPC structure. To handle uncertainties, a radial basis function neural network (RBFNN) will be integrated into each agent's controller, with its weights updated online via a Lyapunov-based adaptive law to ensure stability. The finite-time convergence will be enforced by incorporating terminal attractor dynamics into the error transformation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous distributed control design that guarantees explicit, user-defined transient performance (e.g., convergence speed, steady-state error) for multi-agent encirclement tasks. It will advance the field of distributed nonlinear control by merging PPC, finite-time stability, and neural adaptive control within a relative-measurement-only framework, directly applicable to collision-resilient UAV swarms.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of prescribed performance control with a distributed neural network compensator specifically for a relative-measurement-based multi-agent system, ensuring both predefined transient bounds and finite-time convergence, has not been fully explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Distributed Finite-Time Encirclement Control for Multi-Agent Systems via High-Gain Sliding Modes and Relative Measurements
proposals.motivation: In multi-agent tasks like target encirclement or collision-resilient formation, achieving rapid, robust convergence despite disturbances is critical. Pre-2026 distributed control often trades off convergence speed for robustness, with asymptotic stability leaving systems vulnerable during transient phases. Finite-time stability offers superior performance but remains challenging to guarantee in distributed networks using only local, relative measurements under realistic uncertainties.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a high-gain scaling mechanism with a distributed sliding mode control (SMC) law, designed using only relative distance and bearing measurements, will guarantee finite-time convergence and disturbance rejection for a network of agents performing encirclement or formation tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative distance-based positioning framework from Xie's prior work. A distributed sliding manifold will be defined using agents' relative state errors. A novel continuous control law will be synthesized, combining a high-gain term scaled by a power of the sliding variable to enforce finite-time reaching, and a discontinuous SMC term for robustness against bounded disturbances. Stability and finite-time convergence will be proven using Lyapunov theory and homogeneity concepts, validated in simulation for quadrotor UAVs with collision resilience behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formally verified distributed control framework that ensures finite-time, robust convergence for multi-agent systems using local sensing. It will advance the field by offering a practical synthesis method that bridges the gap between the robustness of SMC and the precise timing guarantees needed for critical cooperative tasks.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration lies in the co-design of the high-gain scaling parameter and the sliding manifold specifically for a distributed, relative-measurement-only architecture, ensuring finite-time stability without requiring global state information or velocity measurements.

proposals.title: Distributed Neural Anti-Synchronization Control for Large-Scale Nonlinear Networks with Uncertainties and Heterogeneous Delays
proposals.motivation: Large-scale interconnected systems, such as swarms of UAVs or smart grids, are composed of uncertain nonlinear subsystems with inherent communication and actuation delays. Pre-2026 control methods often struggle to guarantee scalable, resilient performance in such networks due to the complexity of coupling delays and model uncertainties, which can lead to instability or performance degradation.
proposals.hypothesis: A distributed control framework, leveraging only relative local measurements and neural network-based approximators, can achieve stable anti-synchronization for large-scale networks of uncertain nonlinear agents despite the presence of heterogeneous, time-varying delays.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative distance-based positioning and neural anti-synchronization control from Xie's prior work to a graph-based network topology. Each agent will employ a local radial basis function neural network (RBFNN) to approximate its unknown nonlinear dynamics and delay effects using only relative state information from neighbors. A distributed Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional will be constructed to design delay-dependent adaptive update laws for the RBFNNs and a robust relative controller, ensuring stability across the network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, distributed control architecture for delay-afflicted nonlinear networks, validated through numerical simulations of a 100+ agent system. It will contribute a formal stability proof for the closed-loop network and demonstrate how local relative measurements suffice for global coordination under uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The integration of adaptive neural approximators within a purely relative-information, distributed control scheme to handle heterogeneous delays in large-scale networks is novel, moving beyond prior work focused on smaller groups or simpler, delay-free dynamics.

proposals.title: Distributed Parameter and Bound Identification for Multi-Agent Systems with Uncertain Dynamics via High-Gain Observers and Relative Measurements
proposals.motivation: In distributed control of multi-agent systems (e.g., UAV swarms), agents often operate with significant, heterogeneous model uncertainties, which can degrade the performance of cooperative tasks like encirclement or formation. Pre-2026 methods for robust or adaptive control typically require known bounds on uncertainties or complex online estimators that are difficult to distribute. A systematic, lightweight method to actively identify key uncertainty parameters or their bounds using only local and relative sensory data is a critical unmet need for resilient autonomy.
proposals.hypothesis: The error dynamics and convergence properties of a high-gain observer (HGO), when applied to an agent with uncertain nonlinear dynamics, can be inverted to formulate a feasible distributed optimization problem that identifies bounded parameters of the uncertainty using only relative state measurements and neighbor communication.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative distance-based positioning framework from Xie's prior work. Each agent will employ a standard HGO for its own state estimation, treating lumped uncertain dynamics as an extended state. By analyzing the observer error dynamics and leveraging the property that the estimation error of the extended state is a function of the true unknown parameters, we will formulate a constrained identification problem. This problem will be solved distributively using a consensus-based optimization algorithm, where each agent uses its own HGO output and relative measurements (e.g., from onboard vision or UWB) of neighbor states to collaboratively identify bounds or structured parameters of the group's uncertain dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel distributed identification framework that explicitly bridges high-gain observation theory and cooperative parameter estimation. It will yield a practical algorithm for multi-agent teams to self-calibrate their models of uncertainty, directly enhancing the robustness of existing distributed encirclement and formation controllers. The results will advance the field of resilient distributed control by moving from robust design assuming known bounds to active online identification of those bounds.
proposals.novelty: While HGOs are well-established for state and disturbance estimation, using their intrinsic structure to pose and solve a distributed inverse problem for model uncertainty parameters is a novel direction. The integration of this identification layer with a relative measurement-based positioning framework, core to the researcher's expertise, creates a unique distributed sensing-and-identification paradigm.

proposals.title: Distributed Multi-Agent Encirclement via Discrete-Time Relative Positioning and Impulsive Neural Synchronization Control
proposals.motivation: The researcher's prior continuous-time designs for target encirclement and collision resilience are foundational but assume perfect, continuous communication and control actuation. Real-world implementations on digital platforms (e.g., UAV swarms) are inherently discrete-time or event-triggered, introducing sampling, quantization, and communication delays that can destabilize the system. A rigorous discrete-time or hybrid reformulation is essential for bridging theory to practical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: The core continuous-time control laws for relative distance-based positioning and neural antisynchronization can be discretized using a forward Euler or impulsive control framework, preserving convergence and encirclement properties while explicitly accounting for sampling intervals and intermittent measurements.
proposals.method: We will first derive a discrete-time counterpart of the relative positioning dynamics, ensuring the distributed framework matching is compatible with sampled local measurements. Next, we will design a discrete-time or impulsive neural network controller, replacing the continuous-time antisynchronization dynamics with a discrete update law or state jumps at sampling instants. Stability and performance will be analyzed using discrete-time Lyapunov methods and tools for impulsive systems, explicitly characterizing maximum allowable sampling periods (MASPs) for safe operation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first discrete-time/hybrid system version of the researcher's encirclement control suite, offering explicit stability guarantees under digital implementation constraints. It will deliver practical design rules (e.g., safe sampling rates) for engineers deploying these algorithms on real multi-agent platforms, enhancing their robustness to digital limitations.
proposals.novelty: While discrete-time multi-agent control exists, a direct, stability-preserving translation of this specific neural antisynchronization and relative positioning architecture for encirclement tasks is novel. The integration of impulsive control theory to handle the neural controller's synchronization dynamics under intermittent updates represents a significant advance over simple periodic discretization.

ideas.title: Network-Robust Event-Triggered Control with Predictive Quantization
ideas.description: This research designs a control system that jointly optimizes event-triggered sampling and communication quantization for operation over realistic, imperfect networks. The core innovation is a co-design framework where the event-triggering rule and the quantizer's codebook are dynamically adapted based on an online estimate of the network's current state (e.g., delay, loss probability). By using a predictive quantizer that encodes the likely future state evolution, the system compensates for lost or delayed packets, preventing the instability that plagues naive combinations of event-triggering and quantization. The result is a provably stable control scheme that delivers significant resource savings in bandwidth and energy while maintaining robustness to common network imperfections.

ideas.title: Adaptive Super-Twisting Control with Dynamic Quantization for Chattering Suppression
ideas.description: This research develops a robust control framework that combines a second-order super-twisting sliding mode controller with an adaptive, dynamic quantization strategy. The super-twisting algorithm inherently reduces chattering by integrating a continuous control law, making it more suitable for the discontinuous perturbations introduced by quantization. An online adaptation mechanism adjusts the controller's boundary layer and the quantizer's resolution based on a real-time estimate of the quantization error magnitude and the system's state. This synergistic approach guarantees finite-time convergence and practical stability while significantly mitigating the chattering and energy waste associated with standard SMC under quantized feedback.

ideas.title: Joint Quantization-Delay Operator Analysis via Delay-Dependent Sector Conditions
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework for analyzing control systems subject to simultaneous quantization and time-varying network delays. The core innovation is modeling the combined effect as a single nonlinear operator with delay-dependent sector bounds, rather than treating delays as separate additive disturbances. The analysis employs a tailored Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional that explicitly accounts for how past delayed states influence current quantization error. This yields less conservative stability criteria and enables the co-design of quantizer and controller parameters that are robust to the coupled nonlinear dynamics introduced by networked delays.

ideas.title: Rate-Distortion Theoretic Control: Characterizing the Fundamental Performance-Quantization Limit
ideas.description: This idea adapts the foundational Rate-Distortion theory from information theory to the problem of control under fixed quantization density. The analogous problem is determining the minimum bitrate needed to represent a signal within a fidelity target, solved by deriving the R(D) function. Here, the fixed quantizer density ρ imposes a constraint analogous to a rate constraint. The adapted solution is to formulate and solve for a 'Performance-Quantization' function P(ρ), the dual to the original idea's goal. This involves statistically modeling the relevant signals (state, error) and defining a control cost as the distortion metric. The outcome is a theoretical characterization of the fundamental limit of control performance achievable under any coding/control strategy for a given quantizer, providing a benchmark akin to the Shannon limit in communication.

ideas.title: Kernel-Based Sector Analysis for Robust Control of Uncertain Systems with Continuous Nonlinearities
ideas.description: This research fuses kernel methods with sector-bound analysis to create a novel framework for robust control of systems with both parametric plant uncertainties and complex, continuous nonlinearities in the feedback path. Instead of approximating a nonlinearity (e.g., from a sophisticated quantizer or actuator saturation) with a finite set of linear sectors, kernel-based learning is used to implicitly represent it in a continuous, high-dimensional feature space. Within this RKHS, a 'kernel-induced sector' condition is formulated, allowing the application of robust control theory to derive stability and performance guarantees for the uncertain closed-loop system. This provides a rigorous, data-informed method to handle nonlinearities that lack a simple parametric form, which neither traditional sector bounding nor standalone kernel regression for system identification could achieve alone.

ideas.title: Fast Variational Inference for Stability-Constrained Quantizer Design
ideas.description: This research develops a novel Bayesian framework that unifies fast variational inference for off-grid models with the inversion of sector-bound stability conditions. The core idea is to treat the maximum allowable sector bound for a quantizer not as a deterministic limit but as a latent variable with a prior distribution, influenced by the quantizer's density and the statistical properties of the input signal. The proposed fast, deterministic variational Bayesian solver then infers the posterior distribution of this allowable sector bound from observed system data, enabling joint probabilistic stability analysis and quantizer parameter optimization. This allows for the design of quantized systems that are provably stable with a quantified confidence level, rather than just a binary stable/unstable classification, merging statistical learning with robust control theory.

ideas.title: Rate-Distortion-Optimal Adaptive Gridding for Heterogeneous and Dynamic Sources
ideas.description: This research synthesizes adaptive grid offsets with rate-distortion theory to develop a principled method for optimal quantization in multi-source or time-varying systems. It frames the selection of per-source or time-varying grid offset parameters as an information-theoretic optimization problem, trading off the bit-rate required to describe the adaptive grid structure against the resulting distortion in representing the source signals. This enables the derivation of fundamental performance bounds and practical algorithms that automatically adapt quantization density to the statistical properties and temporal dynamics of each source, a capability absent in fixed-grid or non-theoretic adaptive approaches. The result is a unified theory for efficient sampling and compression in distributed sensor networks, non-uniform analog-to-digital conversion, or dynamic spatial field estimation.

ideas.title: Use Adaptive Gain Scheduling for Dynamic Quantizer Tuning, Inspired by Physiological Homeostasis
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from systems physiology, specifically homeostatic control. The analogous problem is how biological systems maintain stability by adaptively adjusting internal parameters (like hormone levels) via feedback, often modeled as gain scheduling. The adapted solution is to employ an adaptive gain-scheduling controller for dynamic quantizer tuning. This supervisory controller would continuously monitor a defined performance metric (e.g., SQNR, stability criteria) and the system's operating mode. It would then online adjust the quantizer's parameters (e.g., adaptive step-size, dead-zone) according to a pre-designed schedule or adaptation law that is triggered by the current operating conditions, ensuring robust performance and efficiency across dynamic ranges, much like a physiological system maintains homeostasis.

ideas.title: AMP-Guided Adaptive Grid Design for Sparse Recovery
ideas.description: This research direction develops a methodology to co-design an approximate message passing (AMP) inference algorithm and the physical sampling grid on which it operates. The core innovation is to use the state evolution equations of AMP, which characterize its performance under specific measurement matrices, as a constraint within an inverse grid design optimization. The objective is to find the sparsest set of non-uniform sample locations (e.g., in time, frequency, or space) such that the corresponding measurement operator guarantees AMP will converge to a solution meeting a pre-specified recovery error bound. This results in a principled framework for optimal sensor placement or sampling schedule design that is explicitly tailored to the capabilities and structure of modern efficient inference algorithms.

ideas.title: Simultaneous Localization and Calibration via Expectation-Maximization
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard assumption of perfectly known sensor positions in Direction of Arrival (DOA) estimation by drawing an analogy to Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) in robotics. In SLAM, a robot must jointly estimate its own location (akin to DOAs) and the map of its environment (akin to the sensor array geometry) from noisy sensor data. The analogous solution uses iterative probabilistic algorithms like Expectation-Maximization (EM) to break the circular dependency. Adapted to array processing, the research would develop a novel EM-based framework that alternates between estimating the DOAs and source signals (E-step) and refining the estimates of the unknown sensor position errors (M-step), using the received signal data as the sole observable. This allows for the joint calibration of the array and estimation of source directions without prior knowledge of the precise sensor locations.

ideas.title: Model quantizer dynamics as a piecewise-smooth biological switch
ideas.description: The original idea proposes modeling a quantized feedback system as a hybrid automaton. By drawing an analogy from systems biology, where piecewise-smooth hybrid models are used to analyze genetic toggle switches and cellular decision circuits, we can adapt a powerful solution method. The analogous solution uses Filippov convex analysis and reachability tools to study bistability and transitions in biological networks. Adapting this, we model the quantizer as a piecewise-smooth dynamical system where discrete output levels are automaton modes and input threshold crossings trigger transitions. This adapted approach allows the formal analysis of quantizer-induced behaviors—such as limit cycles, chattering, and stability properties—in feedback loops using established tools from hybrid systems theory, providing a rigorous framework for design and certification.

ideas.title: Structure-Aware Conic Sector Bounds for MIMO Systems with Vector Quantization
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for stability analysis of MIMO systems with quantized feedback that directly addresses the conservatism of naive multi-channel extensions. The core innovation is a directional sector characterization of the vector quantizer, which is coupled with a decomposition of the linear MIMO plant dynamics along its principal input-output directions. By formulating the analysis using a tailored Integral Quadratic Constraint (IQC) framework that respects this directional coupling, the method yields stability conditions that are significantly less conservative. The resulting design guidelines explicitly inform how to shape the linear compensator and select quantization density across channels to jointly ensure robust stability with practical performance.

ideas.title: Dual Problem: Source Localization with Quantized DOA Feedback for Performance Bounds
ideas.description: This research investigates the dual problem of determining the best achievable source localization accuracy when the estimated Direction-of-Arrival (DOA) measurements available to a central processor are constrained by a fixed, finite-resolution quantizer. Instead of designing a quantizer for given localization performance, we treat the quantizer's density (e.g., number of bits per measurement) as a fixed resource constraint and seek to characterize the fundamental Cramér-Rao or minimax lower bounds on localization error. The core synergy lies in using robust, potentially off-grid DOA estimation as the input to this analysis, creating a closed-loop understanding of how quantization distortion in the angular domain propagates to ultimate positional error bounds, thereby informing optimal resource allocation in bandwidth-limited sensor networks.

ideas.title: Hybrid Automaton Modeling for Scalable Analysis of Quantized Feedback in Massive MIMO
ideas.description: This research direction develops a scalable hybrid automaton model to analyze and design quantized feedback control for massive MIMO systems. It formally captures the interaction between the discrete quantization events at each antenna element and the continuous-valued wireless channel and precoding dynamics. The core challenge is to create a tractable, hierarchical hybrid model that can predict system stability and optimize quantizer allocation across the array, leveraging spatial channel correlations to reduce feedback overhead. The outcome is a rigorous design framework for ultra-low-bitrate feedback in next-generation cellular networks, balancing analytical guarantees with practical scalability.

ideas.title: Finite-Level Quantizer Analysis with Explicit Rate-Distortion Scaling Laws
ideas.description: This research develops a theoretical framework for static quantizers with a finite number of levels, N, where N is treated as a fundamental resource. The core contribution is deriving explicit, non-asymptotic scaling laws for distortion and other performance metrics as functions of N for various source distributions and quantizer design rules. This allows for the precise characterization of the 'finite-to-infinite' transition, identifying critical values of N needed to approximate infinite-level performance within a specified tolerance. The work provides practical design guidelines and a robust theoretical foundation for system analysis where the infinite-level assumption is untenable.

ideas.title: Joint Calibration of Distributed MIMO Arrays with Sector-Bound Quantized Feedback
ideas.description: This research fuses array self-calibration with MIMO feedback techniques to address a critical bottleneck in distributed MIMO deployment. It develops algorithms to jointly estimate Direction of Arrival (DOA), channel state information, and the unknown physical positions of sensors in a distributed MIMO array, using only the coarsely quantized sector-bound feedback from user equipment. The synergy creates a novel system that can self-organize and calibrate without precise prior knowledge of sensor locations or high-rate feedback links, making large-scale, ad-hoc MIMO deployments practically feasible and robust to calibration errors.

ideas.title: Generalizing Sector Bounds via Topological Network Equivalence Classes
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from electrical circuit theory's treatment of two-port networks, this idea proposes a method to generalize sector bound results for controller-quantizer configurations. In network synthesis, complex circuits are categorized into equivalence classes (e.g., based on impedance matrices and passivity) to derive universal realizability conditions, avoiding exhaustive enumeration. Adapted to this control context, the research would seek to define topological equivalence classes for feedback loops containing quantizers and dynamic controllers, based on properties of the effective nonlinearity and possible loop transformations. The goal is to develop a procedure to classify any new controller-quantizer configuration into a canonical class, thereby automatically inheriting the pre-derived sector bounds and stability guarantees for that entire class, creating a powerful and extensible framework for future analysis.

ideas.title: Co-Design of Adaptive Sampling Grids and Algorithm Configurations for Robust Performance Guarantees
ideas.description: This research introduces a meta-optimization framework for the joint design of data sampling grids and algorithm configurations to meet robust, multi-objective performance guarantees. The system moves beyond a static inverse problem by simultaneously optimizing grid geometry and key algorithm hyperparameters, constrained by a user-defined Pareto frontier of metrics like accuracy, computational cost, and robustness. This co-design approach produces grids that are inherently adaptive and guarantee performance across a family of algorithm instances, not just a single fixed specification. The outcome is a principled methodology for creating efficient, robust sensing or computational strategies where the data collection mechanism and the processing algorithm are synergistically designed.

ideas.title: Biologically-inspired fixed-point neural quantization for embedded real-time systems
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational neuroscience—the efficient, discrete spike coding of biological neurons—to the problem of scaling down models for embedded real-time systems. In biology, neurons communicate via all-or-nothing action potentials (spikes), a form of extreme quantization that is energy-efficient and temporally precise. We propose to develop a low-complexity, fixed-point version of neural networks for real-time deployment by designing a novel quantization scheme inspired by this mechanism. The 'spike-inspired quantization' function will map activations to a very limited set of fixed-point values based on dynamic thresholds, emulating the sparse, event-driven nature of neural firing. This approach inherently minimizes memory footprint, reduces computational cost to simple integer operations, and ensures temporal determinism, making it uniquely suited for strict real-time constraints in embedded systems.

ideas.title: Motion-Induced Triangulation for Single-Array Source Localization Using Temporal DOA Evolution
ideas.description: This system overcomes the inherent range ambiguity of single-array DOA measurements by leveraging sensor platform motion. A single sensor array, mounted on a mobile agent, collects a time series of off-grid DOA estimates. The core innovation is a probabilistic filter that jointly estimates the unknown, possibly moving, source location and refines the sensor's own trajectory by solving the dual problem: constraining the source to lie at the intersection of the time-varying DOA cones. This transforms an ill-posed angular-only problem into a well-posed spatiotemporal estimation task, enabling robust 3D localization without a pre-deployed sensor network.

ideas.title: Event-Triggered Quantized Control via Biological Synaptic Pruning
ideas.description: This idea combines the quantizer sector bound with event-triggered sampling for resource savings by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience. The analogous field is synaptic plasticity, where the brain uses 'synaptic pruning' to eliminate weak, infrequent connections to conserve metabolic resources while maintaining critical function. The adapted solution is to design a control protocol where updates are triggered not just by plant state error, but by a predicted violation of performance bounds based on the quantizer's sector-bound characterization. This creates a dual-scale resource allocation: the quantizer handles fine-grained signal approximation locally, while the network channel is only activated (like a synapse being strengthened) for coarse-grained corrections when necessary to prevent instability, mimicking the efficiency of biological neural networks.

ideas.title: Robust Bayesian Filtering with Student-t Likelihoods for Impulsive Interference
ideas.description: This idea proposes to replace the standard Gaussian noise assumption in Bayesian filtering and estimation problems with a more robust prior to handle impulsive interference, inspired by solutions in financial econometrics. In that field, heavy-tailed phenomena like asset returns are effectively modeled using Student-t distributed errors, often implemented via a scale mixture of normals representation for computational ease. We adapt this solution by developing a robust Bayesian filter where the observation likelihood is Student-t, modeling the heavy-tailed noise. Using the latent variable representation transforms the problem into a conditionally Gaussian one, allowing the adaptation of efficient Kalman-filter-like recursions within a Monte Carlo or variational inference scheme, thereby creating a computationally feasible and robust estimator for systems subject to outliers and impulsive noise.

ideas.title: Runtime-Adaptive Fixed-Point Scaling for Robust Embedded Real-Time Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a low-complexity, fixed-point deployment system enhanced with runtime adaptive quantization. A minimal overhead monitoring unit continuously tracks key activation statistics, enabling dynamic adjustment of per-layer scaling factors to prevent overflow and minimize precision loss. This approach maintains the deterministic execution and hardware efficiency of fixed-point arithmetic while robustly handling variable input data and operational environments. The result is a system suitable for real-time embedded deployment that is far more resilient than a statically scaled model.

ideas.title: Non-Stationary Adaptive DOA via Switching Gaussian Process Experts
ideas.description: This approach models the direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation problem using a mixture of Gaussian process experts to capture non-stationary signal fields. A Dirichlet Process prior automatically infers the number of distinct angular regimes (e.g., different interference patterns or source constellations) from the data. The system performs joint online inference over source directions, regime assignments, and GP hyperparameters, allowing it to adaptively switch between learned models as the acoustic environment changes. This results in robust DOA estimation under dynamic, multi-source conditions where a single transfer GP would fail.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement for Inverse Algorithm Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to grid design from Computational Fluid Dynamics—specifically, Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR)—to the original inverse problem of designing an optimal sampling grid for a given algorithm. In CFD, AMR solves the analogous inverse problem: given a solver and a desired solution accuracy, it designs the coarsest possible computational mesh by starting from a fine grid and strategically coarsening regions where the solution is smooth, guided by error estimators. Adapted to this context, the research would develop a method that, for a fixed algorithm, begins with a dense sampling grid and employs a novel 'algorithmic sensitivity metric' as an error estimator. The grid is then iteratively and locally coarsened in regions where the metric indicates the algorithm's performance is least affected, continuing until further coarsening anywhere would violate a pre-specified performance guarantee. The outcome is a formally derived, non-uniform, coarsest-possible sampling grid that ensures the algorithm meets its performance bounds, directly inverting the traditional sampling-to-performance relationship.

ideas.title: Fundamental Performance Limits of Massive MIMO with Fixed-Resolution Quantization
ideas.description: This research investigates the hard performance ceiling for massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems when the resolution of the data converters (quantizers) is fixed a priori due to cost and power constraints. It moves beyond adaptive algorithms to derive fundamental information-theoretic and control-theoretic bounds on capacity, estimation accuracy, or beamforming gain for an array with hundreds of elements, each connected to a low-resolution quantizer. The core novelty lies in characterizing how the optimal performance scales with the number of antennas under this fixed-quantizer constraint, answering whether sheer array scale can compensate for coarse quantization or if a saturation point exists. This provides crucial guidance for the cost-effective design of next-generation massive MIMO hardware, defining the minimum quantizer resolution needed to harness the gains of a large array.

ideas.title: Structured Correlation Models for Identifiable Off-Grid Source Recovery
ideas.description: This approach addresses the identifiability crisis of coherent off-grid models by integrating a low-rank or factor-structured prior for the source correlation matrix within a sparse Bayesian learning framework. The model assumes that source correlations arise from a small number of underlying latent processes, making the joint estimation of source locations, complex amplitudes, and correlation structure well-posed. This allows for the resolution of highly correlated or coherent sources while preserving the ability to accurately localize them on a continuous domain. The method provides a principled trade-off between model flexibility and the information required for stable inverse solution.

ideas.title: Adaptive Clock Bias Calibration using Multi-Epoch Reference Frame Alignment
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a fixed, universal grid offset parameter by drawing an analogy from geodesy, specifically GNSS positioning. In precise GNSS analysis, the receiver clock bias—analogous to a grid offset—is not treated as a single fixed error. Instead, it is solved for as distinct parameters per satellite constellation (GPS vs. GLONASS) or modeled as a time-varying stochastic process to account for hardware-specific delays and oscillator drift. Adapting this solution, the proposed research would develop methods to estimate the grid offset parameter individually for each source in a multi-source system or model it as a time-varying parameter (e.g., via a Kalman filter or random walk process). This adaptive calibration would improve accuracy by accounting for source-dependent instrumental delays or temporal variations caused by environmental factors or system instability, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all offset assumption.

ideas.title: Adaptive Rate-Distortion for Quantization with Learned Perceptual Distortion
ideas.description: This research develops an adaptive quantization framework that robustly applies rate-distortion theory to real-world data. It combines theoretical bounds with empirical estimation to handle unknown and non-stationary source distributions. The system learns a perceptual distortion metric directly from data or task objectives, moving beyond simple MSE. This results in a practical method that achieves theoretically-informed minimal bitrates while preserving critical information for downstream perception or decision tasks.

ideas.title: Kernel-Based Continuous Dictionary Learning via Analogous Spectral Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry—specifically the Kernel Polynomial Method (KPM) for spectral analysis of quantum systems—to the signal processing problem of continuous dictionary learning for Direction of Arrival (DOA) estimation. The analogous problem in quantum mechanics involves resolving a continuous density of states from a discrete matrix representation using kernel-smoothed polynomial expansions. The adapted solution proposes using kernel-based learning within a similar polynomial expansion framework (e.g., Chebyshev) applied directly to the signal covariance matrix. This method implicitly projects the data into a high-dimensional feature space corresponding to a continuous angle domain, enabling super-resolution DOA estimation without an explicitly discretized dictionary, thus solving the off-grid mismatch problem inherent in traditional sparse methods.

ideas.title: Apply Belief Propagation from Error-Correcting Codes for Off-Grid Sparse Recovery
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core message-passing solution from error-correcting codes—specifically, belief propagation used in decoding LDPC codes—to the signal processing problem of off-grid sparse recovery. In LDPC decoding, BP efficiently infers bits by passing probabilistic messages across a graph to resolve uncertainties. Here, we construct a similar factor graph for a continuous parameter space, where variable nodes represent the potential off-grid components (with parameters like amplitude, frequency, and a binary support indicator), and factor nodes enforce the measurement model. By designing approximate message passing rules that propagate beliefs about these continuous parameters and their likelihood of being active, the algorithm can iteratively and rapidly converge on a sparse, off-grid solution, offering a faster and more accurate alternative to grid-based compressive sensing methods.

ideas.title: Resolving Ambiguity in Blind Array Calibration via Signal-Structure Constraints
ideas.description: This system jointly estimates Directions-of-Arrival (DOAs) and unknown sensor position errors while explicitly preventing the fundamental identifiability failure of the original formulation. It does so by integrating a strong, physics-based constraint derived from the intrinsic structure of the source signals themselves, such as the harmonic relationships in acoustic or narrowband communication signals, or known cyclostationary properties. This structural prior acts as an anchor, breaking the symmetry between possible array geometries and source directions. The resulting algorithm is provably more robust, converging to the correct sensor geometry and DOAs where the original, unconstrained method would fail.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Hierarchical Bayesian Model for Grid Offset Estimation with Auxiliary Data Constraints
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simply allowing grid offsets to vary, by embedding the variation within a constrained, identifiable model. It uses a hierarchical Bayesian framework where source-specific or time-varying offset parameters are treated as latent variables with a prior distribution shaped by real-time, locally-measured auxiliary data related to grid state. This structure strongly couples offset estimation to physical observables, preventing degeneracy with the scientific signal. The result is a robust method that can capture genuine grid dynamics and anomalies while producing reliable, physically-plausible estimates of the primary signal of interest.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Nonparametric Noise Modeling via Hierarchical Bayesian Mixtures
ideas.description: This approach replaces the assumption of a single parametric heavy-tailed distribution with a flexible, data-adaptive prior for the noise model. It uses a hierarchical Bayesian framework with a nonparametric component, such as a Dirichlet Process mixture of Gaussians or symmetric distributions, to infer the noise distribution's shape from the data itself. This allows the model to automatically capture tail behavior, handle heterogeneous or multi-modal interference, and remain robust even when the nature of the impulsiveness is unknown or changes. Inference is performed via scalable variational or Markov Chain Monte Carlo methods to learn the posterior over both the signal parameters and the infinite-dimensional noise model.

ideas.title: Transfer Gaussian Process Regression for DOA via Multi-Task Learning from Geostatistical Kriging
ideas.description: This idea expands transfer Gaussian process regression for DOA by drawing an analogy from multi-task kriging in geostatistics. The analogous solution uses correlated Gaussian processes to model multiple spatial fields jointly, improving predictions for a sparsely sampled target field by borrowing strength from a related, densely sampled source field. Adapted to DOA, the signal field across angles is modeled as a multi-task Gaussian process. A coregionalization kernel captures the correlation between the source and target signal fields (e.g., from different but related acoustic environments or array configurations). During training, data from both environments inform the hyperparameters of this joint model. For inference in the new target environment, the model leverages this learned correlation structure, providing more robust and data-efficient DOA estimates by transferring the underlying spatial structure of the signal field from the source domain.

ideas.title: Fast Variational Inference via Adaptive Mesh Refinement from Computational Fluid Dynamics
ideas.description: This research seed proposes developing a faster, deterministic variational Bayesian solver for the off-grid model by drawing an analogy from adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) techniques in Computational Fluid Dynamics. In CFD, AMR solves complex flow equations efficiently by dynamically refining the computational grid only in critical regions, avoiding the cost of a uniformly fine mesh. Adapted to this context, the algorithm would begin with a coarse variational distribution (e.g., over source locations or parameters) and, during optimization, selectively add finer-scale basis functions or split components only in areas of high posterior density or high local approximation error. This creates an adaptive, multi-resolution variational approximation that concentrates computational resources on the most informative parts of the parameter space, leading to a significant speed-up over static, uniformly fine variational families while maintaining or improving accuracy for the off-grid inference problem.

ideas.title: Low-Overhead Hierarchical Signal Processing for Scalable Massive MIMO via Model-Based Deep Learning
ideas.description: This research designs a scalable signal processing framework for massive MIMO systems that fundamentally addresses the complexity and pilot overhead bottlenecks. It employs a two-stage hierarchical architecture combining low-resolution compressive channel estimation with a model-based deep neural network for data detection, ensuring sub-linear complexity growth with antenna count. A meta-learning outer loop continuously adapts the model parameters to the underlying spatial channel correlation structure, minimizing required pilot symbols. The system is co-designed for implementation on hybrid hardware, enabling real-time operation for arrays with hundreds to thousands of elements by intelligently exploiting channel sparsity and structure.

ideas.title: Scalable Hierarchical Relaxation for Joint Sparsity via Adaptive Non-Convex Regularization
ideas.description: This research develops a computationally efficient framework for joint sparsity that overcomes the intractability of classic structured Bayesian priors. It introduces a novel continuous, non-convex regularizer derived as a tight variational relaxation of a spike-and-slab prior, enabling the use of fast deterministic optimization algorithms like proximal gradient descent. The method features a hierarchical parameterization that automatically learns coupling strengths within and between groups from data. This results in a scalable, model-based approach that retains the statistical benefits of structured priors while achieving computational performance comparable to simple convex penalties like the lasso or group lasso.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Adaptive Kernels for Probabilistic Continuous Dictionary Learning
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the rigidity of pre-defined kernels by formulating a meta-learning objective that jointly optimizes a flexible, parameterized kernel function alongside the continuous dictionary representation. The system employs a deep kernel network where the kernel parameters are adapted based on the task or data distribution at hand, enabling it to learn the appropriate similarity metric for the underlying continuous Degrees of Freedom space. A probabilistic framework using variational inference maintains a distribution over dictionary elements, providing robustness to noise and a measure of uncertainty. This results in a model that can automatically discover complex, non-Euclidean manifold structures in data, leading to more accurate and generalizable representations than a fixed-kernel method.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Tensor Decomposition for Massive MIMO Channel Estimation
ideas.description: This idea addresses scaling algorithms to massive MIMO systems by drawing an analogy from computational chemistry, specifically electronic structure calculations for large molecules. The analogous problem—solving the high-dimensional Schrödinger equation for systems with hundreds of interacting electrons—is tackled using hierarchical tensor decompositions (e.g., Tensor-Train format) to break the exponential complexity. We adapt this solution by modeling the massive MIMO channel covariance matrix as a high-dimensional tensor (incorporating spatial dimensions across the large array, frequency, and user/angle domains). A hierarchical decomposition tree is constructed, factoring the global covariance into a series of lower-dimensional core tensors and transfer matrices that capture interactions at different spatial scales (e.g., sub-array, panel, full array). This structure exploits the channel's inherent locality and limited rank, transforming channel estimation, feedback, and precoding computations into recursive operations on these smaller components. The result is an algorithm whose complexity scales near-linearly with the number of antennas, enabling practical implementation for systems with hundreds or thousands of elements while capturing the full correlation structure.

ideas.title: Adapting NMR Spectroscopy's Coherence Pathway Selection to Off-Grid Source Separation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption of uncorrelated sources in off-grid sparse signal models (e.g., in direction-of-arrival estimation or compressed sensing). Drawing an analogy from Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy, where techniques like phase cycling and pulsed field gradients are used for 'coherence pathway selection' to separate correlated signals from different nuclei, we propose adapting this principle. The adapted research idea involves designing a measurement or estimation framework for off-grid models that does not require source independence. Instead, it would intentionally exploit coherent/correlated structure through a form of 'analytical phase cycling'—using controlled, known modulation patterns or phase relationships across the sensor array or temporal snapshots. This creates a virtual separation mechanism based on the correlation signatures themselves, allowing for the resolution and localization of highly correlated or coherent sources even when their parameters (like directions) do not fall on a pre-defined discrete grid.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Trait Evolution Models for Joint Sparsity Priors
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard Laplace prior for joint sparsity by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology. In phylogenetics, the evolution of binary traits across related species is modeled with structured stochastic processes (e.g., stochastic Dollo models) that account for shared history and complex transition dynamics between states. Adapting this solution, we propose using a phylogenetic trait evolution model as a structured prior. Here, the sparsity pattern across related variables (e.g., coefficients in a regression over time or across brain regions in a network) is treated as a binary trait evolving along a dependency tree or graph. This prior models the probability of a coefficient becoming non-zero (trait gain) or returning to zero (trait loss) based on its ancestors' states, creating a rich, generative prior for joint sparsity that moves beyond simple shrinkage and explicitly encodes the expected structure of sparsity propagation.
